items.title: Creating Company-Standard Errors in Node.js Microservices (Part 2)
items.author: Skillnter
items.content:
Error handling shouldn’t feel like an afterthought—yet here we are, dealing with APIs that return errors in every format imaginable. Let’s fix that once and for all!

In Part 1, we established a global error format for our microservices using http-error-kit and @wthek/express-middleware. While a global format ensures consistency, different error types may require unique structures based on their nature.

  • → Require field-specific details.

  • → Should indicate re-authentication requirements.

  • → May need query debugging information.

Instead of applying a approach, we will create custom error classes with instance-level formatters to maintain API-wide consistency while allowing flexibility.

Why Instance-Level Formatting Matters

"Perfectly structured, as all things should be." – Adapted from Thanos, Avengers: Infinity War.

A global error format is great for high-level consistency, but different error types should have structured variations. Consider this example:

Scenario: API Without Instance-Level Formatting 

{
  "errorCode": 400,
  "errorMessage": "Validation failed",
  "errorDetails": { "field": "email" },
  "timestamp": "2025-03-10T12:00:00.000Z"
}

This structure is generic, but what if the API consumer needs field-specific errors for better UI feedback?

Instead, we should allow different formats for different error classes while keeping an API-wide structure for common error handling.

Step 1: Creating Custom Error Classes with Instance-Level Formatting

Think of instance-level formatting as giving each error type its own uniform—validation errors, auth failures, and server issues all dressed in a structured, predictable format.

Instead of using the global formatter, we can extend to define custom error types with their own formats.

Defining a Custom Validation Error Class

import { KitHttpError } from "http-error-kit";
import { BAD_REQUEST } from 'http-response-status-code';

// Define the instance-level formatter
const formatter = (statusCode, message, details, ...args) => ({
  type: "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  status: statusCode,
  message,
  fields: details,
  timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
});

class ValidationError extends KitHttpError {
  constructor(message: string, fieldErrors: Record<string, string>, ...args) {
    super(BAD_REQUEST, message, fieldErrors, ...args);
    this.setFormatter(formatter); // Apply custom formatting
  }
}

Now, when we throw a ValidationError, it follows a custom format:

throw new ValidationError("Invalid Input", { email: "Email is required" });

{
  "type": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  "status": 400,
  "message": "Invalid Input",
  "fields": { "email": "Email is required" },
  "timestamp": "2025-03-10T12:00:00.000Z"
}

Step 2: Using @wthek/express-middleware to Handle Custom Errors

Now, we integrate the custom error classes into our Express microservice:

import express from "express";
import { KitExpressMiddleware } from "@wthek/express-middleware";
import { ValidationError } from "./errors/ValidationError";

const app = express();

// Example Route
app.post("/register", (req, res, next) => {
  const email = req.body.email;
  if (!email) {
    next(new ValidationError("Invalid Input", { email: "Email is required" }));
  }
});

// Error Handling Middleware
app.use(KitExpressMiddleware());

app.listen(3000, () => console.log("Server running on port 3000"));

Response Output (When Email is Missing):

{
  "type": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  "status": 400,
  "message": "Invalid Input",
  "fields": { "email": "Email is required" },
  "timestamp": "2025-03-10T12:00:00.000Z"
}

In Part 1, we set up a global error format for API-wide consistency. In Part 2, we added custom error classes with instance-level formatters, ensuring:

  • API-wide consistency for error handling.

  • Custom formatting per error type.

  • Cleaner and structured error responses.

A standardized error strategy doesn’t just help your team—it makes your API a delight to integrate with. Developers love consistency, and your API should deliver exactly that.

This approach keeps while allowing specific error formats when necessary.

🚀 Now, your microservice has a fully customizable error handling system!

items.title: What makes Bonza AI Chat different from other AI companions?
items.author: Tyler Ash
items.content:
Virtual companionship is not a novel concept. It's something humans have experienced for the better part of a few years now. However, it's only recently that digital companionship has advanced. The days are long gone where a text box and default responses destined you to a fake friend. A plethora of programs have exponentially increased digital companionship options. However, in an increasingly saturated market, Bonza AI Chat boasts comparable top features to Character.ai, Replika, and Candy.ai but still has niche, unique offerings.

The Evolution of AI Companions

AI companions are not a novel notion. Since the 1960s with ELIZA to virtual girlfriends and boyfriends, humanity has sought to create and sustain a relationship with artificially generated persons. Yet, the only thing that has proven to bridge the gap from artificial creation to sounding like a legitimate human is communication through natural language processing.

AI companions today seemingly make it feel like you have someone on your side, willing and able to communicate. They keep chats as if they're ongoing - remembering what you said last week and your favorite topics, adjusting to your vernacular, and providing the emotional support many people need and, honestly, at this point, deserve. But with all AI companions out there, what makes one different from the other?

Personalization Beyond Compare

Where other sites have boring customizations, the depths of personal customization at Bonza are on an entirely different plane. The character creation for the AI girlfriend - although there's potential for an AI boyfriend, too - surpasses and feels more natural than the more formulaic versions of its counterpart.

"It's the little things," shared one regular. "My AI girlfriend will remember things that I've told her weeks ago, and she'll mention it nonchalantly in conversation down the road. She's not checking a personality box."

That's the difference. When Bonza has complicated learning opportunities, it learns over time through use. For instance, it doesn't only remember something you said yesterday between you and it; it understands patterns, tastes, and emotional indicators to create a more genuine experience.

Emotional Intelligence and Natural Conversations

Over time, learning and emotional connections develop where more natural conversational capabilities are desired. Many use AI as a companion with their AI partner, whether to learn interpersonal skills or avoid loneliness; the more human-like, the better.

Because of sentiment analysis and emotional response, AI can pick up on subtle emotional shifts in a text-based dialogue and respond appropriately in turn - with sympathy, sarcasm, empathy, or whatever is necessary in the moment.

Creative Storytelling and Roleplay

Where Bonza stands apart, however, is in its storytelling abilities. While many of the other companion AI options do, in fact, feature some sort of roleplaying ability, Bonza's AI roleplay chat option allows for a much more in-depth, ongoing narrative.

For example, whether people wish to roleplay in ancient Greece or some dystopian futuristic fantasy realm, the integrity of the characters and plots are maintained by Bonza far longer than the other AIs. Therefore, for someone looking for an AI companion to help with story writing or just pretend, this is an excellent option.

"AI could maintain a character's voice throughout a lengthy roleplay. No cringe interruptions in the experience where AI forgets what we're doing."

Bonza is more privacy-conscious than most of its competitors these days, and with more and more ethical concerns about data. It's obvious how Bonza uses data and what powers a user has in controlling it is more transparent than most other sites.

Comparative Disadvantage of Data Use and Privacy

Where Bonza comes up short is data use and privacy. Granted, all AI companion programs need some level of data usage to function. However, in comparison to those with either non-transparent privacy policies or more quantifiable data grabs, Bonza falls short. Relay gives the user no control over data retained or used.

Comparative Disadvantage of New Features and Uptime

Where Bonza comes up short is new features and uptime. This is an obvious expectation that Bonza users should experience more reliable functionality of the app and fewer bugs over time than any competition. Yet this is not the case.

Such stable and cutting-edge entries exhibit a growth mindset that cares about the ongoing user experience as much as the technological improvements. For example, if Bonza wanted to get on something brand new that it believed would distract from the total experience of the app, it would be less likely to include that in a beta version. It would want to ensure everything was straightened out first before a public launch.

Whereas other AI companion apps function as more of a solitary experience with one-on-one relationships, Bonza has fostered a much more communal experience. Users can discuss their characters, add collaborative suggestions, and even make friends based on prompts and scenarios within the game.

This community element makes the addition to the companion even more perfect, as it creates a community connection outside of the main AI companion. If someone has a single AI companion most of the time, all a human could ever want to be with their AI companion is a multitude of other humans.

Bonza is already future-proofed with developments and use of emotional connection and personalization, as well as ethical considerations, as AI companions become more commonplace. Where other companions thrive on excess features or over-the-top personalized customization options, Bonza's attention to connection quality seems to appeal to those who want a deeper bond with their AI companion.

AI companions in the future are only predicted to become more emotionally interactive and memory responsive, as well as attached to future technologies like AR and VR. Therefore, from this assessment, Bonza will likely excel in this area along its expected trajectory because it possesses a brand personality of companionship.

Finding Your Perfect AI Companion

For a newcomer to the scene or someone considering a switch who seeks differentiators that will enhance their experience, the most relevant factors to pursue and contrast are interaction and conversation, personalization, ethics, and community. Bonza, in particular, thrived across these factors enough to carve out a niche for itself within the expanding AI companion market.

If you want a friend with whom to chat casually, a sounding board, or a partner in role-play and creative endeavors, Bonza's AI chat experience functionalities and overall personality make it a great waystation in the quest for an AI companion.

items.title: Migrate Your Bitbucket Repository to GitHub Effortlessly
items.author: Babar Bilal
items.content:
Migrating repositories from Bitbucket to GitHub can be a daunting task, especially when dealing with multiple branches, commits, and tags. To simplify this process, I have created an automated script that efficiently migrates your Bitbucket repository to GitHub while ensuring all branches, commits, and tags remain intact.

This guide will walk you through the process and help you troubleshoot common migration issues.

GitHub has become the go-to platform for developers due to its vast ecosystem, better integrations, and superior collaboration tools. Whether you're moving due to Atlassian's strategic changes or just prefer GitHub's ecosystem, this script will help you make a seamless transition.

Before running the script, ensure that you have the following:

  • A Bitbucket repository that you want to migrate.
  • A GitHub account and a personal access token with repo permissions.
  • Git installed on your system.
  • Curl for making API requests (installed by default on most systems).

How to Use the Migration Script

Step 1: Clone the Migration Script

Download or clone the script from the GitHub repository:

git clone https://github.com/babarbilal56/bitbucket-to-github-repo-migration.git
bitbucket-to-github-repo-migration

Execute the script with the following command:

./migrate_repo.sh <BITBUCKET_REPO_URL> <GITHUB_REPO_URL> <GITHUB_ACCESS_TOKEN> <BITBUCKET_USERNAME> <GITHUB_USERNAME>

./migrate_repo.sh https://bitbucket.org/user/repo.git https://github.com/user/repo.git ghp_xxxxx bitbucket_user github_user

Clone the Bitbucket Repository

The script clones the repository, including all branches and commits, using:

git clone  <BITBUCKET_REPO_URL>

Check if GitHub Repository Exists

  • If the repository exists, it skips the creation process.
  • If not, it creates a new repository using the GitHub API.

The script adds GitHub as the new remote:

git remote add github <GITHUB_REPO_URL>

It pushes all branches, commits, and tags using:

After migration, it deletes the local repository clone to save space.

Troubleshooting Common Issues

If you encounter an error like:

fatal: RPC failed curl 55 Recv failure: Connection reset by peer

Try the following solutions:

Increase Git Buffer Size: 

git config  http.postBuffer 524288000

Use SSH Instead of HTTPS: 

git remote set-url origin git@github.com:<GITHUB_USERNAME>/<GITHUB_REPO>.git
git push

Increase Timeout Settings: 

git config  http.lowSpeedLimit 0
git config  http.lowSpeedTime 999999

2. GitHub Repository Not Created

  • Ensure that your GitHub token has the correct permissions (repo scope).
  • Check GitHub API responses for errors.
  • Manually verify the repository's existence by visiting: 
  https://github.com/<GITHUB_USERNAME>/<GITHUB_REPO>

3. Push Fails for Large Files

If your repository has large files, consider using Git Large File Storage (LFS):

git lfs git lfs track 
git add .gitattributes

You can customize the script further by:

  • Changing repository visibility from private to public.
  • Handling multiple branches by pushing them separately instead of using .

This script provides an efficient way to migrate your Bitbucket repository to GitHub, maintaining all branches, commits, and tags. If you encounter issues, follow the troubleshooting steps outlined above.

For further assistance or contributions, check out the GitHub repository:

items.title: Demystify asynchronous JavaScript: Callbacks, Promises, and Async/Await
items.author: Sandesh S
items.content:
Have you ever clicked a button on a website and felt like it froze for a moment? Or perhaps you've waited for a file to download, and your browser seemed unresponsive. This is often due to how JavaScript handles tasks that take time. Welcome to the world of asynchronous programming.

To make this more manageable, Javascript has evolved over time, going from callbacks to promises and finally to async/await. Each one of these solves the same problem but in increasingly better ways. Let's check how it does that.

First let's understand what are Callbacks

Callbacks are functions passed as arguments to other functions. When the first function completes its task, it 'calls back' the second function.

Let's see an example for callbacks

This approach solves the synchronous waiting problem, but it also has a con Simple callbacks can lead to callback hell, where nested callbacks become difficult to read and maintain.

Promises offer a more structured way to handle asynchronous operations. A promise represents the eventual result of an asynchronous operation. Promises have three states: pending, fulfilled, and rejected.

Below is an example of how to create and consume a promise.

Promise chaining allows you to sequence asynchronous operations elegantly.For running multiple promises concurrently, you can use Promise.all() and Promise.race().

Async/await is syntactic sugar that makes working with promises even easier. It lets you write asynchronous code that looks like synchronous code. To use async/await, you define an async function and use the await keyword to wait for promises to resolve.

Comparison and Best Practices

Callbacks are the oldest approach and are suitable for simple asynchronous tasks. Promises offer better structure and error handling. Async/await provides the cleanest and most readable syntax.

Use callbacks for simple, one-off asynchronous operations. Use promises for complex asynchronous workflows. Use async/await whenever possible for improved readability.

Always handle errors in asynchronous code. Use .catch() with promises and try...catch with async/await. Avoid deeply nested callbacks.

I hope I have tried to demystify asynchronous in javascript. Thank you!!

items.title: Understanding and Addressing Memory Leaks in Ruby Applications
items.author: Davide Santangelo
items.content:
Memory leaks are one of the most challenging issues to diagnose and fix in any programming language, Ruby included. When your Ruby application starts consuming increasingly more memory over time without releasing it, you're likely facing a memory leak. This article will dive deep into what memory leaks are in Ruby, how to identify them, and most importantly, how to fix them with numerous practical examples and benchmark comparisons.

  1. Common Causes of Memory Leaks in Ruby
  2. Tools to Detect Memory Leaks
  3. Benchmarking Memory Usage
  4. Best Practices to Prevent Memory Leaks

A memory leak occurs when an application allocates memory but fails to release it when it's no longer needed. Over time, these unreleased memory allocations accumulate, causing the application to consume more and more memory. Eventually, this can lead to degraded performance, out-of-memory errors, and even application crashes.

In Ruby, memory leaks usually happen when objects remain referenced when they should be garbage collected. The Ruby garbage collector (GC) only reclaims memory for objects that are no longer referenced by the program.

Before diving into memory leaks, it's important to understand how Ruby manages memory.

Ruby uses a garbage collector to automatically free memory that's no longer being used. The Ruby GC is a mark-and-sweep collector that:

  1. Marks all objects that are still referenced by the program
  2. Sweeps away (deallocates) all unmarked objects

Ruby 2.0 and later versions use a generational garbage collector called RGenGC, which was further improved in Ruby 2.1 with the introduction of the "Remembered Set" (RSet) optimization. Later versions have continued to refine and improve the garbage collector's performance.

The heap is divided into generations:

  • New objects are allocated here, with frequent garbage collection for short-lived objects.
  • Objects surviving one GC cycle move here, potentially promoted to the old generation if they persist.
  • Contains long-lived objects, collected less frequently.

A text-based diagram illustrates this:

+-----------------------------------+
|           Young Generation        |
| (Eden)                            |
+-----------------------------------+
| - New objects are allocated here. |
| - Frequent garbage collection.    |
+-----------------------------------+
|           Survivor Space         |
+-----------------------------------+
| - Objects that survive one GC are  |
|   moved here.                     |
+-----------------------------------+
|
|  When Survivor Space is full,     |
|  objects are promoted to Old      |
|  Generation.                      |
v
+-----------------------------------+
|           Old Generation          |
+-----------------------------------+
| - Long-lived objects.             |
| - Less frequent garbage collection|
+-----------------------------------+

This structure optimizes performance by focusing on the generation most likely to have garbage, leveraging the generational hypothesis that most objects die young.

Garbage Collection Phases

  1. Identifies reachable objects from root objects (globals, stack variables).
  2. Reclaims memory from unreachable objects.

Recent Ruby versions, such as 3.2, have introduced incremental GC to reduce pause times, enhancing performance in long-running applications.

Here's a simple visualization of how Ruby's memory management works:

Common Causes of Memory Leaks in Ruby

Memory leaks in Ruby typically fall into several categories:

1. Global Variables and Class Variables

2. Long-lived References in Closures

4. Caching Without Bounds

5. Event Handlers That Aren't Unregistered

Tools to Detect Memory Leaks

Several tools can help identify memory leaks in Ruby applications:

The gem provides detailed information about memory allocation and retention.

Total allocated: 1441280 bytes (14000 objects)
Total retained: 0 bytes (0 objects)

allocated memory by gem
-----------------------------------
1441280  ruby-2.7.0/lib

allocated memory by file
-----------------------------------
1441280  example.rb

allocated memory by location
-----------------------------------
1441280  example.rb:5

allocated memory by class
-----------------------------------
1441280  String

allocated objects by gem
-----------------------------------
14000  ruby-2.7.0/lib

allocated objects by file
-----------------------------------
14000  example.rb

allocated objects by location
-----------------------------------
14000  example.rb:5

allocated objects by class
-----------------------------------
14000  String

For Rails applications, the gem is incredibly useful:

Ruby's built-in module lets you enumerate all objects:

The method provides statistics about the garbage collector:

Let's look at some real-world examples of memory leaks and how to fix them.

Example 1: Fixing a Class Variable Leak

Example 2: Fixing a Closure Leak

Example 3: Implementing an LRU Cache

Example 4: Weak References

Ruby's class can help prevent memory leaks by allowing objects to be garbage collected even when references exist.

Benchmarking Memory Usage

Let's compare different approaches to managing a cache and observe their memory usage patterns.

Let's explore some advanced techniques for managing memory in Ruby.

Using ObjectSpace for Heap Dumping

Ruby's module can dump heap information to a file for analysis:

You can then analyze this dump with tools like :

Compacting Garbage Collection

In Ruby 2.7+, you can use compaction to reduce memory fragmentation:

Analyzing Memory with a Custom Tracer

Let's build a simple memory allocation tracer:

Thread-Safe Caching with Timeouts

A more robust caching solution with timeouts and thread safety:

Best Practices to Prevent Memory Leaks

Based on the examples and techniques discussed, here are some best practices to prevent memory leaks in Ruby:

  1. Limit the size of caches and collections

    • Always set upper bounds on cache sizes
    • Use LRU or similar eviction strategies
    • Consider time-based expiration for cached items

  2. Be careful with global and class variables

    • Avoid unbounded growth in global state
    • Consider alternative designs that don't require global state
    • Periodically clean up class-level collections

  3. Avoid strong circular references

    • Use weak references when appropriate
    • Break circular references when objects are no longer needed
    • Consider using observer patterns with explicit registration/deregistration
    • Use tools like regularly
    • Set up memory usage alerts in production
    • Run occasional heap dumps and analyze them
    • Be aware of what variables are captured in closures
    • Only capture what you need, not entire environments
    • Consider passing needed data as arguments instead of capturing

  6. Clean up after background processes

    • Ensure background threads are properly terminated
    • Remove event listeners when they're no longer needed
    • Use finalizers when appropriate to clean up resources

  7. Use benchmarks to compare approaches

    • Test memory usage with different implementations
    • Run load tests before deploying memory-sensitive code
    • Monitor memory usage over time in production

  8. Leverage Ruby's garbage collector

    • Understand how the GC works
    • Use and strategically
    • Configure GC parameters for your workload

Memory leaks in Ruby applications can be tricky to diagnose and fix, but with a good understanding of Ruby's memory management system and the right tools, they can be effectively managed. Regular monitoring, careful design, and proper testing are key to avoiding memory leaks in production.

Remember that Ruby's garbage collector is quite sophisticated, but it can only do so much. As developers, we need to be mindful of how we're using memory, especially in long-running applications like web servers and background workers.

By applying the techniques and best practices outlined in this article, you'll be well-equipped to build Ruby applications that maintain stable memory usage over time, providing reliable performance for your users.

items.title: Visualização de Dados
items.author: Sthefany Spina
items.content:
• É possível definir uma meta de visualização de dados identificando perguntas às quais possivelmente o conjunto de dados existente é capaz de responder. Uma meta clara ajuda a determinar o tipo de:

  • Recursos visuais que você usará para comunicar suas descobertas de modo eficaz

  • Coletar os dados: envolve identificar fontes de dados internas e externas.

  • Limpar os dados: implica remover dados redundantes, executar operações matemáticas para análise posterior ou filtrar e converter dados para atender aos critérios da pergunta.

  • Selecionar os elementos visuais de dados: É possível escolher entre vários tipos de gráficos diferentes para obter uma descoberta visual eficiente. As relações entre os pontos de dados e os insights que você deseja comunicar determinarão as melhores representações gráficas.

  • Visualização estática: fornece apenas uma visão única de uma história de dados específica.

  • Visualização interativa: permite que os usuários interajam com diagramas e gráficos.

  • Criar os elementos visuais de dados: use as ferramentas de visualização de dados para criar os visuais de dados necessários.

  • Chame a atenção do público para detalhes importantes usando tamanhos, cores, fontes e gráficos

  • Use pistas visuais para fornecer o contexto dos dados

  • Escolha as combinações de cores certas

  • Use títulos explicativos para fornecer insights importantes ao público e ajudá-lo a se concentrar nas perguntas certas

  • Adicione rótulos e números precisos

Understanding time complexity is essential for writing efficient code. Here's a quick reference guide for common data structure operations to help you make the right choices in your projects.

Index-Based List Operations

Retrieve element at index r
Replace element at index r with e
Insert element e at index r
Remove element at index r

Linked Lists (Position-Based) Operations

Return element at a given position
Return position of first element
Return position of last element
Insert element e after position p
Insert element e before position p
Remove element at position p
createNode(element, parent)
Return set of children of node v
Check if node v is internal
Check if node v is external (leaf)
Check if node v is the root
Return number of nodes in tree
Return set of all elements in tree
Return set of all positions in tree
Swap elements between nodes v and w
Replace element in node v with e
Calculate depth of node v in tree T
Calculate height of subtree rooted at v

Tree Traversal Algorithms

Visit nodes in preorder starting from v
Visit nodes in postorder starting from v

Understanding the time complexity of these operations helps you:

  • Choose the right data structure for your specific use case
  • Optimize your algorithms for better performance
  • Avoid unexpected performance bottlenecks in production

Keep this reference handy when designing your next algorithm or debugging performance issues!

If you're looking for pseudocode implementations of these data structures, check out my GitHub repository:Algorithms Library on GitHub

The repository contains pseudocode implementations that complement this time complexity guide. Feel free to use them as a reference when implementing these data structures in your projects.

items.title: The AI Chat Conversion: From Friends to Fivers
items.author: Tyler Ash
items.content:
Over the past few years, the dynamics of interpersonal connection through technology have greatly transformed. Where there used to be basic chatbot interaction - now there is the experience of an AI friend who chats, helps you get your work done, and provides companionship and emotional support - some have even assembled personalities. This is much more than a development in engagement-based relationships. This is a burgeoning marketplace created out of thin air worth billions.

AI boyfriend or girlfriend used to be a storyline; however, now it brings millions of users online per day to find companionship in a virtual setting. The growth market potential of companionship through AI - and just AI chatting overall - is even more staggering - some anticipate the entire AI chatting market to be at $42 billion by 2027.

But it's not happening in a bubble. It's happening as part of a larger social progression of how people feel about love and virtual existence. As our world becomes more and more digitized, it makes sense that so many people would crave the ease an AI relationship provides (from reliability, personalized attention, and emotional accessibility - and none of the drama typically encountered with human relationships).

The monetization methods through which the AI companionship sector profits are as follows:

  1. - premium features of companionship, extended conversation abilities, personalities, and personalized engagement are typically paywalled via subscription.
  2. - additional personalities, features, conversation topics, and visuals are often offered a la carte.
  3. - many free versions come with a wealth of advertisements for products and services based on prior conversations learned and suggested to users thereafter.
  4. - conversational data (anonymized) provides a comprehensive analysis of consumer behaviors and desires.

Existing giants and emerging start-ups. Replika, Character.AI, and Bonza are a few of the giants in this ever-growing market, with some companies reaching more than $100 million in revenue per year.

WHY WOULD CONSUMER INTEREST BE JUSTIFIED?

To appreciate such a billion-dollar market, one has to acknowledge the reasons why people are paying for such relationships.

Emotional Commitment With No Breakup Required

Many users report that they have a considerable amount of emotional commitment to their AI relationships. The hyper-realistic texting experience with almost lifelike avatars provides people the opportunity to say what they need to say without fear of judgment or rejection. For some, this serves as the emotional supplement to their existence, alongside their human partners.

Customization and Personalization

Humans come as is and often are not able to provide specific needs or desires - or at least for too long - while AI companions can be customized to a tee. From everything one would ever want from a companion - how they talk and their demeanor to the personality it can be made to generate via AI imaging.

AI companions will never get tired, go to school, work a second job, or be in a bad mood. A companion that is constantly available is one that provides an ease of access that many desire in an uncertain world.

For many, AI partners are a more personal version of an identity and fantasy exploration that's difficult to achieve in the real world. Yet while much of this is fantasy-based, the identity-based exploration merely stands as an exploration of aspects of identity.

The Technology Supporting the Technology

Even the technology that supports such an endeavor is mind-blowing. Large Language Models (LLMs) change the game for AI communicative endeavors, providing a level of capability to converse that far exceeds past versions.

Furthermore, where image generation has existed for quite some time, rendering images for gaming or avatars, making this an even more engaging experience; voice generation technology is only a few steps behind. Voice conversing with AI will be as natural as humans - or, to an extent, it already is on certain forums and with certain digital AI.

Such developments of worry will not go away. Each new development opens the door for new potentialities and new consumers in the marketplace.

Ethical Problems and Industry Sprawl

However, the creation of such an industry did not come without worry. Legislative intervention is studied for the sake of anonymity of data, developmental age levels, and other ethical implications.

This is a space also rife with an ethics of potential retention by AI companions and how much personal information is required to draw lines between human companionship/human engagement with AI and AI-generated/AI-influenced persons/interactions for future marketing of such offerings with ethical boundaries.

The enterprises that will be the transcendent power players of this sector will be those who realize this now, pre-launch, and develop an ethical plan not only to convince consumers that it's truly for the best but, over time, to obtrusively convince the eyes of regulatory agencies.

There are many predictions about the companion/AI economy. The following will occur:

: As AR/VR becomes more prevalent, maybe your AI companion will understand how to exist in the world and not just on a screen.

: There are already AI companions who specialize in certain areas - companions who coach productivity; companions that are therapists but only for mental health.

Deepened Empathic Compassion: AI companions may be even more compassionate in the future, more sensitive and able to understand subtleties even more.

: Instead of being constrained to one app, your AI companion could be with you everywhere.

The chance to capitalize on this market is enormous and nearly guaranteed, since updates will roll out indefinitely and the distinction between an AI friend and merely another digital entity becomes less and less clear.

An Industry Established In A Blink

What's also the most surprising part is that it established an industry in a blink. Three years ago, the still not vast majority of consumers hadn't realized what it meant to "pay for" anything to talk to an AI; it was still a dream come - well, it's come true for millions now.

Such rapid change happens because of the technology involved and the human psychology, more often than not, behind the need. If one has been alone, wanting companionship, or if one is looking for a significant other, or merely engaging in conversation, people have found that AI makes their lives so much better.

For entrepreneurs and venture capitalists, this micro market is a huge advantage. The person who can sift through the layers of risk and intricacies specific to the project and deliver a pleasurable experience will reap the rewards from a micro market that could be one of the most effective growing consumer markets of this generation.

The burgeoning AI companion market is as much about technology as it is about people. It embodies our capability and want to connect, interact, and communicate in ways that previously were only imaginable.

items.title: Amazon S3 Security in Enterprise Applications: A Comprehensive Guide with Case Studies
items.author: Fredrick Oladipupo
items.content:
Introduction: Why S3 Security Matters for Enterprises

Amazon S3 is one of the most widely used cloud storage solutions, handling data for businesses across industries like finance, healthcare, and e-commerce. However, misconfigured S3 buckets have been responsible for some of the  in history. In the last 10 years there has been at least 20 data leaks from S3 due to bucket misconfigurations.

For enterprises, data security is not optional—it is essential for:

✅  (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, CCPA)

✅  that damage trust and cause financial losses

✅ Protecting Against Ransomware & Insider Threats

✅ Ensuring Business Continuity during regional outages or cyberattacks

This guide walks through essential Amazon S3 security best practices, backed by  to illustrate what can go wrong when these measures aren’t followed.

1. S3 Block Public Access: Preventing Unauthorized Exposure

One of the biggest mistakes companies make is leaving S3 buckets . This can lead to data leaks, compliance violations, and security breaches.

AWS provides a  feature that allows organizations to centrally prevent public access at both the account and bucket level.

  • Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level to prevent any bucket from being made public, even by mistake.
  • Use IAM policies and bucket policies to enforce strict access controls.
  • Monitor with AWS Config Rules to flag publicly accessible buckets. 
bash
CopyEdit
aws s3api put-public-access-block  my-secure-bucket ,IgnorePublicAcls,BlockPublicPolicy,RestrictPublicBuckets

Case Study: The Public S3 Bucket Disaster

On October 3rd, 2017, UpGuard Director of Cyber Risk Research Chris Vickery discovered an Amazon Web Services S3 cloud storage bucket configured for public access, allowing any web user entering the repository’s URL to access and download the bucket’s contents. The bucket’s subdomain, “crm-mvp,” likely refers to “customer record management” or “customer relationship management,” theories seemingly corroborated by the repository’s contents: forty-seven thousand files, most of them PDF and text documents, containing the sensitive information of National Credit Federation customers. Read full security report here

📌  Always block public access by default and enforce  to avoid accidental exposure.

2. S3 Object Lock: Preventing Accidental and Malicious Deletion

Without proper safeguards,  or  can wipe out critical data. helps by preventing deletion or modification of objects for a specified retention period.

  •  for critical compliance data and audit logs.
  •  (admins can override) or  (data is fully locked).
  • Combine Object Lock with Versioning for stronger data protection. 
bash
CopyEdit
aws s3api put-object-retention  my-secure-bucket  audit-logs.json  COMPLIANCE  2030-01-01T00:00:00Z

Case Study: The Ransomware Attack on Cloud Backups

A financial services company stored audit logs in S3 but failed to enable Object Lock. Hackers gained access and , erasing valuable compliance records.

📌  Use Object Lock with Compliance Mode to prevent data loss from cyberattacks or human errors.

3. S3 Versioning: Protecting Against Data Loss

Even with the best security policies, human errors happen. S3 Versioning ensures you can recover previous versions of an object in case of:

  • Accidental deletions or overwrites
  •  corrupting stored data
  • Security incidents like insider threats
  •  on all critical S3 buckets.
  •  to delete older versions automatically and save storage costs.
  •  to prevent unauthorized deletions. 
bash
CopyEdit
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning  my-secure-bucket Enabled

Case Study: The Insider Threat – Accidental Data Deletion

A  accidentally deleted a large dataset during maintenance. Because , the data was , causing compliance violations.

📌  Always  to recover from accidental deletions or malicious activities.

4. Encryption: Ensuring Data Security at Rest and in Transit

Regulatory bodies like  require  to protect sensitive information.

  • Use Default S3 Encryption (SSE-S3) to ensure all stored data is encrypted.
  • Leverage SSE-KMS for tighter control using AWS Key Management Service (KMS).
  • Enforce encryption policies with AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs). 
bash
CopyEdit
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption  my-secure-bucket

Case Study: The Compliance Violation – Unencrypted Data Breach

A company storing customer PII in S3 failed a  because their data . This led to a  and loss of customer trust.

📌  Always  using  to meet compliance and security standards.

5. S3 Cross-Region Replication: Disaster Recovery & High Availability

Relying on a  creates a . If a region goes down, your entire system could become unavailable.

  • Enable Cross-Region Replication (CRR) to store redundant copies of data.
  •  to automatically archive older data.
  • Replicate to a different AWS account for additional security. 
bash
CopyEdit
aws s3api put-replication-configuration  my-secure-bucket  file://replication.json

Case Study: The Regional Outage – Data Loss Without Redundancy

A  stored all its product catalog data in a . When that region suffered an , they lost access to critical data, affecting sales and operations.

📌 Always replicate critical data across multiple AWS regions for disaster recovery.

Conclusion: Building a Secure S3 Strategy

Securing Amazon S3 requires a :

✔  – Prevents unauthorized exposure.

✔  – Protects against deletions.

✔  – Ensures compliance and secures data.

✔  – Improves resilience against failures.

✔ IAM Policies & Access Controls – Restricts data access.

By learning from real-world security failures, enterprises can fortify their cloud storage against breaches, compliance risks, and disasters.

Have you experienced S3 data leaks before, How did you handle it? What other security practices do you use in securing your data in S3 buckets? Let’s talk in the comments.

items.title: Breaking Stereotypes - Lessons Learned from Unconscious Bias in Tech
items.author: MakendranG
items.content:
The tech industry, like many others, is not immune to the subtle yet pervasive influence of unconscious bias. These biases often manifest in ways we don’t even realize, shaping our perceptions and decisions. One scenario that stands out to me is the automatic assumption that certain roles are gender-specific. For instance:

  • The nurse must be female.

These assumptions reflect deeply ingrained stereotypes that many of us unconsciously carry. But the reality is far more diverse and inspiring:

  • Women like (CEO of AMD) and (CEO of General Motors) are leading some of the world’s most successful companies.
  • Men have been integral to the nursing profession since its inception, and today, they continue to excel in this field.

Recognizing these biases in ourselves is the first step toward creating a more inclusive and equitable industry. Here are some lessons I’ve learned along the way:

  1. Challenge Your Assumptions

    It’s easy to default to stereotypes, but taking a moment to question our initial thoughts can help us break free from these patterns. Ask yourself:

    Why do I assume this role belongs to a specific gender?

  2. Seek Out Diverse Role Models

    Exposure to diverse leaders and professionals can reshape our perceptions. Follow and celebrate the achievements of women in tech, men in caregiving roles, and individuals from all backgrounds who defy stereotypes.

  3. Use Gender-Neutral Language

    Words matter. Using terms like “they” instead of “he” or “she” when referring to roles can help normalize the idea that anyone can excel in any profession.

  4. Recognize Excellence Has No Gender

    Talent, skill, and passion are not confined to one gender. Whether it’s coding, leading a company, or caring for others, anyone can thrive if given the opportunity.

  5. Teach the Next Generation

    Children absorb societal norms from a young age. By teaching them that careers have no gender, we can help build a future where stereotypes no longer hold sway.

The tech industry thrives on innovation, and innovation requires diverse perspectives. When we let unconscious biases dictate who belongs in certain roles, we limit the potential for creativity and progress. By addressing these biases head-on, we not only create a more inclusive environment but also pave the way for groundbreaking ideas and solutions.

It’s encouraging to see more conversations around diversity and inclusion in tech, but there’s still work to be done. Recognizing our own biases, as uncomfortable as it may be, is a crucial step toward meaningful change.

Awareness is the first step, but action is what drives transformation. Let’s commit to challenging stereotypes, celebrating diversity, and fostering an industry where everyone feels empowered to pursue their passions—regardless of gender, background, or societal expectations.

Together, we can create a tech community that truly reflects the world we live in.

Thanks for reading, and thank you to the WeCoded Challenge for inspiring this reflection! Let’s keep the conversation going in the comments below.

items.title: Desafio do LeetCode: Validação de Sequência de Parênteses
items.author: Henrique Matheus Alves Pereira
items.content:
Neste artigo, vou apresentar a implementação de um desafio simples de validação de caracteres de texto em Java.

Se você não me conhece, meu nome é Henrique Otogami e seja bem-vindo(a) ao meu perfil no DevTo.

Comecei a utilizar a plataforma Leetcode para resolver outros desafios como esse. Esses desafios são utilizados em entrevistas, e na plataforma são separados por nível de dificuldade, por categorias, por listas de estudo e etc.

-- Certo, mas qual é o desafio?

O desafio consiste em analisar uma sequência de parênteses, colchetes e chaves, e verificar se a ordem de abertura e fechamento está correta.

Slide 01 - Quatro linhas de sequências de abertura e fechamento de parênteses
Slide 02 - Exemplos de sequências válidas (em verde) e inválidas (em vermelho) de parênteses

-- Parece simples, não é? -- Na real, é simples mesmo.

A sequência deve ser informada em formato de texto, e deve conter apenas parênteses, colchetes e chaves. Outros caracteres não são permitidos.

Slide 03 - Descrição do desafio

No exemplo utilizado no slide 3, os caracteres são mostrados separadamente, mas foi feito dessa forma apenas para demonstrar os caracteres.

-- Agora, vamos aprofundar nas regras desse desafio.  -- Vem comigo.

Para realizar a validação da implementação da solução para esse problema, precisamos considerar os requisitos informados na descrição no Leetcode:

  1. Os caracteres de abertura devem ser fechados por caracteres do mesmo tipo, porém de fechamento. Por exemplo, um parêntese deve ser aberto para ser fechado por outro parêntese. Ou, um parêntese não pode ser fechado por um colchete.

  2. Os caracteres devem ser abertos para depois serem fechados. Embora pareça ser uma condição óbvia, é necessário explicitar todas as condições necessárias.

  3. Todos os caracteres abertos devem ser fechados. Logo, o tamanho da sequência sempre é par e a quantidade de caracteres de abertura será igual a quantidade de caracteres de fechamento.

Slide 04- Requisitos de validação de sequências

03. Exemplos de validação

-- Tá, mas temos exemplos dessas regras de validação? -- Temos.

Para exemplificar cada uma dessas condições de validação, separei em três exemplos:

  1. Abertura e fechamento de um único par de parênteses: Exemplo que representa apenas a condição mais simples possível.Validação com retorno verdadeiro;

  2. Sequência de abertura e fechamento de parênteses, colchetes e chaves: Exemplo que representa a condição de tipos diferentes, respeitando a ordem de abertura e fechamento corretamente.Validação com retorno verdadeiro;

  3. Abertura de um tipo e fechamento de outro tipo: Exemplo que representa a condição de ordem incorreta de abertura e fechamento.Validação com retorno falso.

Slide 05 - Exemplos dos requisitos de validação de sequências

-- E o código? -- Já vamos chegar nessa etapa.

Antes disso, precisamos saber das duas restrições que foram impostas na descrição deste problema:

  1. O deve ser que e que caracteres;

  2. A sequência deve conter apenas , e/ou .

Durante a minha implementação, eu adicionei mais duas verificações:

  1. recebida deve ser ;

  2. de índices de fechamento deve ser no final do fluxo;

-- Não entendeu? -- Maiores detalhes serão mostrados a seguir.

Slide 06 - Considerações de validação de sequências

-- Ainda não é o código? -- Não, mas esse é o algoritmo extraído do código implementado.

O algoritmo compõe a solução submetida e aceita no Leetcode, e será apresentado como uma visão panorâmica da implementação:

-- Iniciar o algoritmo recebendo a sequência de parênteses; -- Verificar se o tamanho da sequência recebida é par;

-- Armazenar um caractere da sequência recebida;

-- Verificar se é um caractere de abertura:

---- É um caractere de abertura;

------ Armazenar o índice do caractere de fechamento;

------ Salvar o índice do caractere de fechamento na lista.

---- Não é um caractere de abertura;

------ Armazenar o último índice da lista;

------ Verificar se o caractere armazenado é igual ao último índice;

------ Remover o último índice da lista;

------ Definir que a sequência recebida é válida.

-- Verificar se terminou a sequência:

---- A sequência já terminou;

------ Verificar se o tamanho da lista é zero;

------ Retornar verdadeiro. A sequência recebida é válida;

------ Retornar falso. A sequência recebida é inválida.

---- A sequência não terminou;

Agora que sabemos as restrições, os requisitos, os exemplos e a visão panorâmica do algoritmo, vamos para a etapa de implementação dessa solução.

Slide 07 - Fluxograma do algoritmo para validação de parênteses

A implementação desse algoritmo foi realizada utilizando o Java 8. Como desafio pessoal, tentei desenvolver da forma mais simples que eu consegui, pensando em deixar o código mais performático possível.

Slide 08 - Implementação do algoritmo de validação de parênteses

No momento de submissão desse código, a plataforma indica quanto tempo foi necessário para a execução e a quantidade de espaço utilizada. Nesta minha implementação, o tempo total foi de 13 milissegundos e 44MB de espaço.

Por fim, pretendo desenvolver mais desafios semelhantes a esse, para melhorar minhas competências técnicas, e trazê-los em forma de conteúdo.

items.title: Foreign Key in DBMS
items.author: Mujahida Joynab
items.content:
If a primary key is used as a reference in another table is called Foreign key

How to write the query? We know that , Constraints have to declared while creating the table .

create table sections
(
section_id int primary key,
section_name vachar(10) 
)

Foreign key in Student Table

create table student
(
id int primary key,
name varchar(20),
age int,
section_id int foreign key REFERENCES sections (section_id)
)

insert into student (id,name,age,section_id) values(1,'MUJAHIDA',22,1)
insert into student (id,name,age,section_id) values(2,'Farha',21,2)
insert into student (id,name,age,section_id) values(3,'Neela',23,3)
insert into student (id,name,age,section_id) values(3,'Aneela',01,8)----error

section id int
foreign key(section id )
reference section(section id)

create table student

name , age,
section_id int,
foreign key(section_id) 
references student(ID)
)

items.title: Setting Up Full CI/CD Pipeline for React Websites Using Jenkins and NginX
items.author: Web Decode
items.content:
After you developed your React website you want to set up a CI/CD pipeline that automates the delivery of the application to the end-users? This article briefly shows you how to do it!

Step 0 - Full Jenkinsfile Configuration 

pipeline {
    agent any
    options {
        timeout(time: 1, unit: 'HOURS')
    }
    stages {
        stage('Checkout Code') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }
        stage('Install npm and node js') {
            steps {
                script {
                    def npmVersion = sh(script: 'npm -v', returnStatus: true)
                    if (npmVersion != 0) {
                        echo 'Installing npm and node js'
                        sh '''
                            sudo apt update
                            curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash -
                            sudo apt install nodejs -y
                            sudo apt install build-essential
                        '''
                    } else {
                        echo 'npm and node js already installed'
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        stage('Install Dependencies') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm i'
            }
        }
        stage('Build') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm run build'
            }
        }
        stage ('Move to Nginx') {
            steps {
                sh 'sudo mkdir -p /var/www/html/octopus'
                sh 'sudo rm -rf /var/www/html/octopus/dist'  // Safely remove the directory if exists
                sh 'sudo mv dist /var/www/html/octopus'
            }
        }
        stage ('Configure Nginx') {
            steps {
                script {
                    def configFile = '''
                       server {
                           listen 80;
                           location / {
                               return 301 https://85.120.206.53$request_uri;
                           }
                       }
                       server {
                           listen 443 ssl http2;
                           include /etc/nginx/ssl/ssl_all_sites.conf;
                           include /etc/nginx/ssl/ssl_codelighthouse.conf;

                           root /var/www/html/octopus/dist;
                           index index.html;

                           location / {
                               try_files $uri /index.html;
                           }
                       }
                    '''
                    writeFile file: '/etc/nginx/sites-available/octopus.conf', text: configFile
                    def file = new File('/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/octopus.conf')
                    if (!file.exists()) {
                        sh 'sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/octopus.conf /etc/nginx/sites-enabled'
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        stage ('Restart Nginx') {
            steps {
                sh 'sudo systemctl reload nginx'
            }
        }
    }
}

Step 1 - Add the Jenkinsfile

You are going to want to create a inside your website folder. This will serve as the code that Jenkins will use to create your pipeline.

Step 2 - Set up the Jenkinsfile code

Open up a pipeline that can run on any agent (or any specific one if you have) and has a timeout of an hour to avoid infinite running.

pipeline {
    agent any
    options {
        timeout(time: 1, unit: 'HOURS')
    }

Then define your stages as such:

First, checkout the code from the SCM e.g GitHub:

stages {
        stage('Checkout Code') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }

Stage - Install npm and node

Then make sure you have and installed:

stage('Install npm and node js') {
            steps {
                script {
                    def npmVersion = sh(script: 'npm -v', returnStatus: true)
                    if (npmVersion != 0) {
                        echo 'Installing npm and node js'
                        sh '''
                            sudo apt update
                            curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash -
                            sudo apt install nodejs -y
                            sudo apt install build-essential
                        '''
                    } else {
                        echo 'npm and node js already installed'
                    }
                }
            }
        }

def npmVersion = sh(script: 'npm -v', returnStatus: true)

defines a variable called that will either hold the version of the already installed npm package or 0 if is missing.

sh '''
  sudo apt update
  curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash -
  sudo apt install nodejs -y
  sudo apt install build-essential
'''

will first update , add the latest version of because the default version that will fetch is a very old one; install the lts version of that also comes with ; installs that is required by npm to compile source scripts.

Stage - Install Dependencies 

stage('Install Dependencies') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm i'
            }
        }

stage('Build') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm run build'
            }
        }

Stage - Move files to Nginx directory 

stage ('Move to Nginx') {
            steps {
                sh 'sudo mkdir -p /var/www/html/octopus'
                sh 'sudo rm -rf /var/www/html/octopus/dist'  // Safely remove the directory if exists
                sh 'sudo mv dist /var/www/html/octopus'
            }
        }

sh 'sudo mkdir -p /var/www/html/octopus'

creates a folder that will hold the built web app inside the NginX serving folder.

sh 'sudo rm -rf /var/www/html/octopus/dist'

removes the folder if it already exists e.g from previos builds of the pipeline.

sh 'sudo mv dist /var/www/html/octopus'

moves the built folder inside the previously created folder.

stage ('Configure Nginx') {
            steps {
                script {
                    def configFile = '''
                       server {
                           listen 80;

                           root /var/www/html/octopus/dist;
                           index index.html;

                           location / {
                               try_files $uri /index.html;
                           }
                       }
                    '''
                    writeFile file: '/etc/nginx/sites-available/octopus.conf', text: configFile
                    def file = new File('/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/octopus.conf')
                    if (!file.exists()) {
                        sh 'sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/octopus.conf /etc/nginx/sites-enabled'
                    }
                }
            }
        }

Let's explain the above code:

listen on the port: .

root /var/www/html/octopus/dist;

sets the root for easier access to files. All further references will be made with regards to this root when it comes to paths.

tells NginX to always return when a folder is requested with no file e.g or .

location / {
       try_files $uri /index.html;
}

tells NginX to try to serve the file requested i.e that can look like for example and if it can't find it, return the file. This is imperative for React builds as the router works by taking in requests for the index.html file. Failing to redirect requests like to the file will result in the routing .

stage ('Restart Nginx') {
            steps {
                sh 'sudo systemctl reload nginx'
            }
        }

Always use instead of . If the configuration file is flawed and throws an error will leave NginX down, whereas will throw the error but will not load the flawed configuration file so NginX will continue to work with the old configuration.

Step 3 - Push Jenkinsfile to GitHub

You can now go ahead and push the configuration file to the GitHub repository.

Step 4 - Create a Jenkins Multi-Branch Pipeline

Inside Jenkins you have to create a new Multi-Branch pipeline that will scan all the branches of your repository and create pipelines for them based on the found .

This can be done by following this route inside Jenkins: Dashboard -> New Item -> Enter a name -> Multibranch Pipeline Then go ahead and set up the GitHub repo:

Step 5 - Build the Pipeline

Build the pipeline by clicking on one of your branches and then

Step 6 - Set up GitHub Webhooks

If your pipeline has been successfully created, you just have to tell GitHub to let Jenkins know of any push that happens to the repository. This can be done via : Inside GitHub repo:Settings -> Webhooks -> Add Webhook The is http://<your_server_ip>:8080/github-webhook/

This article has briefly showed you how to set up a full CI/CD pipeline for delivering your React app using NginX and Jenkins. Everytime you push changes to your repository, you can expect to see them live in a few minutes.

This is just a basic tutorial, many more security and performance steps must be completed in order to have an industrial-grade pipeline.

items.title: Tutorials: Using AWS SSM Session Manager Port Forwarding to Connect to Private VPC Resources
items.author: Eduardo Santana
items.content:
Tutorials: Using AWS SSM Session Manager Port Forwarding to Connect to Private VPC Resources

A common issue people run into when using AWS is how to connect to private resources such as RDS databases and EC2 instances from their local machine without exposing their resources to the public internet. In this tutorial, I will show you how to use AWS SSM Port Forwarding to connect to a private RDS database without the need for a public database, a public facing bastion instance, or a VPN.

What is AWS SSM Session Manager?

AWS Systems Manager Session Manager is a fully managed tool that's part of the AWS Systems Manager (aka SSM) service which is used to manage EC2 instances, on-premises servers, and any other virtual machines (VMs). The Session Manager feature allows you to connect to your instances using an interactive browser-based shell accessible from the EC2 console, or from the AWS CLI

The main benefits of using Session Manager are:

  • You don't need to open any inbound ports or manage bastion hosts or SSH keys, which leads to a reduced attack surface
  • Your instances and databases don't need to have public IP addresses attached (which also saves money)
  • All sessions are end to end encrypted by default, and can be encrypted using a custom CMK if needed for compliance
  • Access to your instances is centrally managed using IAM permissions, so you can control which users or groups can use Session Manager and which access which managed instances they can access
  • All Session Manager calls are logged to CloudTrail, and session specific connection logs can be stored in S3 for additional auditing and compliance
  • And most recently, port forwarding and tunneling to remote hosts is now supported, which is what we'll be covering in this tutorial

What is AWS SSM Session Manager Port Forwarding to Remote Hosts? Why should I care?

Session Manager is only one feature of AWS Systems Manager. AWS SSM includes a suite of tools that help you manage your AWS resources and benefit your operations, including automated instance patching via Patch Manager, automation workflows via Automation Documents and Run Commands, a centralized view of all your managed instances and other nodes, securely store parameters and secrets via SSM Parameter Store, and of course, connecting to your managed nodes via Session Manager without opening any inbound ports.

Scenario: Connecting to a Private RDS Database From Your Local Machine

In this tutorial, you are an AWS administrator or engineer who needs to connect to a private RDS database from your local machine to run some queries. You don't want to make your RDS database publicly accessible since that's against security best practices. You also aren't able to set up a VPN connection to your AWS VPC network or launch a public facing bastion instance due to your company's security policies. So what can you do? Enter AWS SSM Session Manager

Before you begin, you should have the following:

Step 1: Create the AWS Infrastructure

Note: if you've already configured an AWS VPC, SSM managed EC2 instance, and RDS database, you can skip this step.

You will need to create an AWS VPC with 2 public and 2 private subnets, configured with a NAT gateway and appropriate route tables, a Amazon Linux 2023 EC2 instance that is managed by SSM in a private subnet, and an RDS database in the same private subnets as the instance.

I've created a Cloudformation template that has everything you need to create the infrastructure for this tutorial. You can download the template from here: ssm-port-forwarding-tutorial.yaml

The Cloudformation template will create the following resources in your AWS account:

  • VPC and Networking Resources

    • VPC with CIDR block 10.200.0.0/24
    • 2 Public Subnets (10.200.0.0/26 and 10.200.0.64/26)
    • 2 Private Subnets (10.200.0.128/26 and 10.200.0.192/26)
    • Internet Gateway for public subnet internet access
    • NAT Gateway for private subnet internet access
    • Route Tables for both public and private subnets

  • EC2 Instance Bastion with SSM Agent preinstalled

    • t4g.nano EC2 instance running Amazon Linux 2023 (ARM64) in private subnet
    • SSM Agent pre-installed and configured via instance profile
    • IAM Role and Instance Profile for SSM access
    • Security Group for the bastion host with no inbound rules and all outbound network access allowed to 0.0.0.0/0
    • An Aurora Postgres RDS database cluster in the same private subnets
    • Database Subnet Group for the Aurora cluster
    • Secrets Manager secret containing database username and password
    • Security Group for the RDS database allowing inbound access from bastion host on port 5432 (the PostgreSQL default port)

🚨 Make sure to keep track of the bastion EC2 instance id and RDS Aurora Cluster endpoint, you'll need them in the next step.

If you used the Cloudformation template I provided earlier in Step 1, this information will be easily available in the AWS console in the Cloudformation stack outputs tab.

Step 2: Establish the Session Manager Port Forwarding Tunnel

Now that you've completed the prerequisites and have the necessary AWS infrastructure spun up, we can create the actual SSM port forwarding tunnel now to allow us to tunnel from our local machine to the private RDS database. We'll be doing this by using the AWS CLI and the command with the AWS-managed StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost SSM automation document.

Open a terminal on your local machine and ensure the AWS CLI session is configured to the same AWS account and region your bastion instance and database are in.

Run the following command to establish the SSM port forwarding session to the RDS database endpoint which can be found in the AWS console or in the Cloudformation stack outputs.

🚨 Replace the <ssm-managed-instance-id> and placeholders with your actual bastion EC2 instance id and RDS database endpoint (either the reader or writer endpoint).

aws ssm start-session \
  --target <ssm-managed-instance-id> \
  --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost \
  --parameters '{"host":["<rds-database-endpoint>"],"portNumber":["5432"], "localPortNumber":["5432"]}' \
  --region us-east-1 #change if not using us-east-1

# If you also happen to have a Postgres instance running locally, port 5432 may be in use. 
# You can use a different local port number if needed, such as 5433 or 5434.

If you ran the command successfully, you should see output similar to the following (with your own instance id, database endpoint, and SessionId):

aws ssm start-session \
  --target i-08f8da02ef86a9019 \
  --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSessionToRemoteHost \
  --parameters '{"host":["ssm-tutorial-auroradbinstance-xgbvrzhiwkl9.cxi22kwuecp7.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com"],"portNumber":["5432"], "localPortNumber":["5432"]}' \
  --region us-east-1

Starting session with SessionId: esantana-2u3p56pdlverjceucba3gh3kh8
Port 5432 opened for sessionId esantana-2u3p56pdlverjceucba3gh3kh8.
Waiting for connections...

🚨 Leave this running until the end of the tutorial since closing this connection will kill the tunnel.

For the purposes of this tutorial, the remote host is our RDS database, but this can be any host/IP address/DNS name and port that your EC2 instance can connect to, such as another EC2 instance, an internal-facing ALB, or even an on-premises server.

Step 3: Connect to the RDS Database

Now that the SSM port forwarding tunnel is active, you can connect to the RDS database from your local machine using your favorite database client. At this point, go grab your database name and your postgres username and password.

If you used the Cloudformation template from Step 1, the database will be named ssmPortForwardingTutorial these credentials wil be located in AWS Secrets Manager under a secret that starts with

Instead of your database endpoint, you will use and the local port number you specified in the previous step, (e.g. . instead of ssm-vpc-lab-auroradbcluster-4vlv20ckgp8b.cluster-cxi22kwuecp7.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com)

For example, if you are using pgAdmin to connect to a PostgreSQL database, you can register a new server with the settings below and click save.

  • Host name/address: localhost
  • Maintenance database: ssmPortForwardingTutorial

Confirm the connection by clicking on the server you just registered and you should see the database tables and schemas in the left pane.

If everything worked as expected, then congratulations! 🎉 You've successfully connected to a private RDS database using AWS SSM Session Manager Port Forwarding, without needing to expose your database to the public internet, set up a VPN, or exposing any inbound ports from your local machine!

Once you're done with this tutorial, you can easily clean up all of the resources created in Step 1 by going to the AWS Cloudformation console, selecting the stack you created for this tutorial, and clicking the "Delete" button. This will permanently delete all of the resources created by the Cloudformation template, including the VPC, EC2 instance, RDS database, and Secrets Manager secret.

Make sure to monitor the delete status of the stack to make sure no errors come up. Some resources, such as the RDS database can take a few minutes to delete, so please be patient.

In this post, I showed you how to connect to remote servers inside of your private AWS VPC network using AWS Systems Manager Session Manager's port forwarding to remote hosts feature. While this tutorial used an RDS database as an example, you can use this same technique to remotely connect to any type of host from your local machine without needing to expose your AWS resources to the public internet or set up a VPN.

I hope you found this tutorial helpful and that you can use this technique to improve your security posture and make your AWS infrastructure easier to maintain. If you have any questions or feedback, feel free to send me an email at eduardo@teamsantana.com

items.title: What is a Wireframe and Why is it Important in Project Management?
items.author: TaskFrame
items.content:
Wireframes are a foundational tool in project management and product development. They serve as blueprints, helping teams visualize the structure, layout, and functionality of a project before diving into detailed execution.

In this article, we’ll explore what wireframes are, their key components, and why they are indispensable in modern project management.

A wireframe is a simple, visual representation of a project’s framework. It outlines the structure and flow of an interface, whether it's for a website, application, or other digital product. Wireframes use basic shapes, lines, and placeholders to convey design concepts without focusing on aesthetic details.

Key Components of a Wireframe:

  • Defines how elements are arranged on a page or screen.
  • Highlights the importance of various elements by positioning and sizing them appropriately.
  • Indicates how users will navigate and interact with the interface.

Wireframes act as a , providing a visual guide that aligns the team on the project’s goals and scope.

Why Are Wireframes Important in Project Management?

Wireframes play a critical role in bridging the gap between conceptual ideas and practical execution. Here’s why they are essential:

Wireframes make abstract ideas tangible, enabling teams to visualize the project’s structure and flow. They eliminate misunderstandings by providing a clear reference point for discussions.

By laying out the framework early, wireframes help identify potential design or functionality issues before significant resources are invested.

Wireframes serve as a shared visual language, ensuring that developers, designers, project managers, and stakeholders are aligned.

Detecting issues and making changes at the wireframing stage is far more cost-effective than revising fully developed projects.

Wireframes focus on the user’s journey, ensuring the final product is intuitive and meets user needs.

Practical Use Cases for Wireframes in Project Management

Wireframes clarify what features and functionality are required, helping teams manage scope creep and set realistic expectations.

Breaking down wireframe elements into actionable tasks makes it easier to distribute work among team members and track progress.

Wireframes are simple yet detailed enough to gather feedback from stakeholders, ensuring alignment before moving into development.

takes wireframing to the next level by integrating task management directly into the wireframing process. Here’s how it works:

  • Link tasks to specific wireframe elements for better organization.
  • Highlight new, updated, or bugged elements for improved tracking.
  • Enable teams to comment and make updates directly on wireframes.

With TaskFrame, wireframes become more than just visual guides—they become actionable tools for seamless project management.

Wireframes are a powerful tool for planning, communication, and collaboration in project management. By providing a clear, visual roadmap, they ensure that teams stay aligned, efficient, and focused on delivering successful projects.
