items.title: Understanding SAP HANA Architecture: Components & Functions
items.author: Aravindh Ramu
items.content:
SAP HANA (High-Performance Analytic Appliance) is an in-memory database and application development platform designed to process high volumes of data in real time. Unlike traditional databases, SAP HANA combines transactional and analytical processing on a single platform, making it a preferred choice for enterprises seeking speed and efficiency.

Key Components of SAP HANA Architecture

SAP HANA's architecture consists of several essential components that work together to optimize data processing and storage. Below are the main components:

The Index Server is the core of SAP HANA and is responsible for processing queries and transactions. It contains:

SQL & MDX Processors – Handle database queries.

Transaction Manager – Ensures data consistency.

Session & Connection Manager – Manages user connections.

Persistence Layer – Provides data recovery and durability.

The Name Server manages the topology of the SAP HANA system and contains metadata about the database landscape. It helps in:

Keeping track of which database runs on which server.

Managing distributed data across multiple nodes.

The Preprocessor Server handles text indexing and extraction tasks for text analytics in SAP HANA. It allows efficient search operations within unstructured text data.

The XS Engine (Extended Services Engine) acts as an application server within SAP HANA, allowing web-based applications to connect directly to the database without requiring an external application server.

This server collects performance data and system information, which helps in monitoring and optimizing SAP HANA’s efficiency.

  1. SAP HANA Studio & Web IDE

These are graphical user interfaces (GUIs) used for:

SAP HANA Studio – A powerful client tool for database administration, modeling, and user management.

Web IDE – A web-based tool used for developing applications on SAP HANA.

Functions & Benefits of SAP HANA

SAP HANA is built to provide superior performance, real-time analytics, and simplified data management. Here are some key functions and benefits:

  1. Real-Time Data Processing

SAP HANA processes massive datasets in real-time, enabling businesses to make faster decisions and gain instant insights.

Unlike traditional disk-based databases, SAP HANA stores data in RAM, significantly increasing processing speed.

It supports advanced features like predictive analytics, machine learning, and text mining, making it ideal for data-driven enterprises.

  1. Simplified Data Management

SAP HANA eliminates data redundancy by integrating transactional and analytical workloads into a single system.

  1. Cloud & On-Premise Deployment

SAP HANA can be deployed on-premise, in the cloud, or in a hybrid model, providing flexibility based on business needs.

SAP HANA's architecture is designed to deliver unparalleled performance, efficiency, and flexibility for modern enterprises. Whether you are a business looking to optimize data processing or an IT professional exploring SAP HANA for career growth, understanding its core components and functions is crucial.

items.title: Python: History, Capabilities, and Future in Backend Development
items.author: Joseph Ayeni
items.content:
: Python was created by Guido van Rossum at the National Research Institute for Mathematics and Computer Science (CWI) in the Netherlands.   : Guido wanted to create a successor to the ABC programming language, which was capable of exception handling and interfacing with the Amoeba operating system. He also aimed for a language that was easy to read and write.   : Python 0.9.0 was released, incorporating features like classes with inheritance, exception handling, and functions.

: Introduced list comprehensions and a garbage collection system.   : A major, backward-incompatible release designed to rectify fundamental flaws in the language.   Python 2 End-of-Life (2020): Python 2 support officially ended, solidifying Python 3 as the standard.

: Python's design emphasizes code readability, and its syntax allows programmers to express concepts in fewer lines of code than would be possible in languages like C++ or Java. This is often summarized by "The Zen of Python" (PEP 20).   Capabilities in Backend Development:

Python has become a powerhouse in backend development due to its versatility and rich ecosystem. Here's why:  

: A high-level web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design. It's known for its "batteries-included" approach, providing built-in features like an ORM, admin interface, and security features. : A microframework that provides a lightweight and flexible foundation for building web applications. It gives developers more control and is ideal for smaller projects or APIs.: A modern, fast (high-performance), web framework for building APIs with Python 3.7+ based on standard Python type hints.  

: For making HTTP requests, essential for interacting with APIs.   : For database interactions, providing an ORM for object-relational mapping.   : A popular PostgreSQL adapter for Python.   : For asynchronous programming, enabling efficient handling of concurrent tasks.   : While primarily used for data science, these libraries can be valuable for backend tasks involving data processing and analysis.

Readability and Simplicity: Python's clean syntax reduces development time and makes code easier to maintain.   Large and Active Community: A vast community provides extensive support, libraries, and resources.   Cross-Platform Compatibility: Python runs on various operating systems, simplifying deployment.   : With frameworks like Django and FastAPI, Python can handle high-traffic applications.   : Python integrates well with other technologies and services.   : Python is very well suited for building microservices.  

Future of Python in Backend Development:

Python's future in backend development looks bright, driven by several factors:

Continued Growth in AI and Machine Learning: Python's dominance in AI/ML is driving its adoption in backend systems that require these capabilities.Rise of Asynchronous Programming: Asyncio and frameworks like FastAPI are enabling Python to handle concurrent tasks more efficiently, improving performance.   : Python's ease of use and extensive library support make it well-suited for building cloud-native applications.   : FastAPI's focus on API development aligns with the growing trend of building API-driven applications.Microservices and Serverless: Python's flexibility and scalability make it a strong choice for building microservices and serverless functions.   : There is ongoing work to make python run efficiently in the browser using web assembly, this could lead to more full stack python applications.: The increased use of type hinting, and the improvements to it, make python more reliable, and easier to maintain.

: While Python has improved, it's generally slower than compiled languages like C++ or Go. However, for many web applications, the performance difference is negligible.   Global Interpreter Lock (GIL): The GIL can limit the performance of multithreaded Python programs. However, this is less of a concern for I/O-bound tasks, which are common in web development.Javascript in the frontend: While python (libraries like PyScript for frontend experiments) can be used full stack, javascript still dominates the frontend. This means that many full stack developers will still need to learn javascript. In conclusion, Python's combination of simplicity, versatility, and a thriving ecosystem ensures its continued relevance and growth in backend development. It's a powerful tool for building a wide range of web applications, from simple APIs to complex, data-driven systems.

items.title: How to use FPGA to complete balance detection?
items.author: Hedy
items.content:
Using an FPGA to complete balance detection involves designing a system that can measure and analyze forces, weights, or other parameters to determine whether an object is balanced. This is commonly used in applications like weighing scales, load cells, robotics, and industrial automation. Below is a step-by-step guide to implementing balance detection on an FPGA:

1. Define the Balance Detection Requirements Input Signals:

  • Determine the type of sensors used (e.g., strain gauges, load cells, accelerometers).
  • Define the number of sensors and their placement (e.g., four sensors for a four-corner balance system).
  • Decide what constitutes "balance" (e.g., equal weight distribution, tilt angle within a threshold).
  • Define the output format (e.g., LED indicators, digital display, or communication interface).

Specify the required precision for balance detection (e.g., 0.1% accuracy).

2. Sensor Interface and Signal Conditioning Sensor Interface:

  • Connect the sensors to the FPGA using analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) or digital interfaces (e.g., I2C, SPI).
  • For analog sensors (e.g., strain gauges), use an external ADC or an FPGA with built-in ADC capabilities.
  • Amplify and filter the sensor signals to remove noise and improve accuracy.
  • Use operational amplifiers (op-amps) or programmable gain amplifiers (PGAs) for signal conditioning.

3. FPGA Design for Balance Detection ADC Interface:

  • Implement an ADC controller in the FPGA to read sensor data.
  • Use a state machine or dedicated IP core to manage the ADC interface.

Digital Sensor Interface:

Implement I2C, SPI, or UART interfaces to communicate with digital sensors.

Calibration:

  • Calibrate the sensor readings to account for offsets and scaling factors.
  • Store calibration coefficients in FPGA memory (e.g., BRAM or flash).

Apply digital filters (e.g., moving average, FIR, or IIR filters) to smooth the sensor data.

Compare the sensor readings to determine balance.

For example, in a four-corner balance system:

  • Calculate the difference between opposite sensors.
  • Check if the differences are within a predefined threshold.

Threshold Detection:

Implement logic to compare the processed data against balance thresholds.

Generate output signals (e.g., LEDs, alarms, or motor control signals) based on the balance status.

4. Implementation on FPGAa. Hardware Description Language (HDL) Write the design in Verilog or VHDL.

  • Digital filter (e.g., FIR or IIR).

b. Simulation and Verification

  • Use simulation tools (e.g., ModelSim, Vivado Simulator) to verify the design.
  • Test edge cases (e.g., unbalanced loads, noisy sensor data).

c. Synthesis and Implementation

  • Synthesize the design using FPGA tools (e.g., Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus).
  • Optimize for resource utilization and timing.

5. Example: Four-Corner Balance Detection

  • Four load cells are placed at the corners of a platform.
  • Each load cell measures the weight at its corner.
  • The FPGA calculates the balance by comparing the weights.

Read data from four ADCs connected to the load cells.

Apply calibration coefficients to the raw ADC data.

Apply a moving average filter to smooth the data.

  • Compare the weights at opposite corners (e.g., front-left vs. back-right, front-right vs. back-left).
  • Check if the differences are within a threshold.
  • Light an LED if the platform is balanced.
  • Activate an alarm if the platform is unbalanced.
verilog

module balance_detection (
    input clk,
    input rst,
    input [11:0] adc_data1, adc_data2, adc_data3, adc_data4, // 12-bit ADC data
    output reg balanced
);
    // Parameters
    parameter THRESHOLD = 50; // Balance threshold

    // Filtered data
    reg [11:0] filtered1, filtered2, filtered3, filtered4;

    // Moving average filter
    always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin
        if (rst) begin
            filtered1 <= 0;
            filtered2 <= 0;
            filtered3 <= 0;
            filtered4 <= 0;
        end else begin
            filtered1 <= (filtered1 + adc_data1) >> 1;
            filtered2 <= (filtered2 + adc_data2) >> 1;
            filtered3 <= (filtered3 + adc_data3) >> 1;
            filtered4 <= (filtered4 + adc_data4) >> 1;
        end
    end

    // Balance detection logic
    always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin
        if (rst) begin
            balanced <= 0;
        end else begin
            // Check if opposite corners are within threshold
            if ((filtered1 - filtered3) < THRESHOLD &&
                (filtered3 - filtered1) < THRESHOLD &&
                (filtered2 - filtered4) < THRESHOLD &&
                (filtered4 - filtered2) < THRESHOLD) begin
                balanced <= 1; // Balanced
            end else begin
                balanced <= 0; // Unbalanced
            end
        end
    end
endmodule

6. Testing and Validation Hardware Testing:

  • Connect the FPGA to the sensors and verify the balance detection.
  • Test with known weights and unbalanced conditions.

Measure accuracy, response time, and stability.

Dynamic Threshold Adjustment:

Allow the threshold to be adjusted dynamically based on environmental conditions.

Add a UART, SPI, or Ethernet interface to send balance data to a host system.

Use a display or LEDs to provide real-time balance status.

Using an FPGA for balance detection involves interfacing with sensors, processing the data, and implementing logic to determine balance. By following the steps above, you can design a robust and efficient balance detection system tailored to your application. FPGAs are ideal for this task due to their flexibility, parallel processing capabilities, and ability to handle real-time data processing.

items.title: Understanding gRPC: A Modern Approach to API Design
items.author: CodeWithVed
items.content:
In the evolving landscape of API development, gRPC has gained traction as a high-performance alternative to traditional RESTful services, particularly in scenarios requiring speed and real-time capabilities. Developed by Google and now an open-source project under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), gRPC offers a fresh take on how applications communicate. So, what is gRPC, and why might it be the right choice for your next project? Let’s explore.

gRPC is a modern, open-source Remote Procedure Call (RPC) framework designed to simplify communication between client and server applications. Unlike REST, which focuses on resources and HTTP verbs, gRPC is service-oriented, allowing developers to define methods that clients can invoke remotely as if they were local functions. It leverages for transport and Protocol Buffers (protobuf) as its interface definition language, delivering efficiency and flexibility across multiple programming languages.

At its core, gRPC revolves around —collections of methods with defined inputs (requests) and outputs (responses). For example, a might include methods like or , each with explicit parameter and return types.

gRPC’s power lies in its robust feature set. Here’s what defines a well-implemented gRPC API:

    • gRPC uses Protocol Buffers to define services and data structures. Protobuf is a compact, strongly typed serialization format that ensures consistency between clients and servers.
    • : A message might include fields like , , and , guaranteeing structured data exchange.
    • Built on HTTP/2, gRPC benefits from features like multiplexing (handling multiple requests over one connection), header compression, and server push, outperforming the HTTP/1.1 foundation of many REST APIs.
    • gRPC offers four communication styles:
      • : One request, one response (like a typical API call).
      • : One request, multiple responses (e.g., live data feeds).
      • : Multiple requests, one response (e.g., data uploads).
      • : Simultaneous request and response streams (e.g., chat systems).
    • This versatility suits real-time use cases exceptionally well.
    • With support for languages like C++, Java, Python, Go, and more, gRPC enables seamless communication between services written in different tech stacks.

  5. Authentication and Security

    • gRPC includes built-in SSL/TLS support and integrates with authentication protocols like OAuth and JWT, ensuring secure data transfer.
    • gRPC employs predefined status codes (e.g., , ) and supports detailed error messages, simplifying debugging and error management.

gRPC brings several compelling benefits to the table:

    • Thanks to HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers, gRPC delivers low-latency, high-throughput communication, often surpassing REST in efficiency.

  • Strong Typing and Code Generation

    • Protobuf enforces a clear contract, and gRPC tools auto-generate client and server code, cutting down on manual coding and errors.
    • Native streaming support makes gRPC ideal for real-time applications like live updates or interactive systems.

  • Language Interoperability

    • Developers can mix and match languages across services without integration headaches.
    • Load balancing, timeouts, and cancellation come standard, reducing reliance on external tools.

Considerations When Using gRPC

Despite its strengths, gRPC isn’t a one-size-fits-all solution. Here are some trade-offs to consider:

    • Compared to REST, gRPC setup can be more intricate, particularly for teams new to Protocol Buffers or HTTP/2.
    • Direct gRPC calls from web browsers are challenging due to HTTP/2 and protobuf limitations. offers a workaround by using a proxy, but it adds another layer to manage.
    • Mastering Protocol Buffers and gRPC concepts requires an upfront investment in learning.

gRPC excels in scenarios that leverage its unique strengths:

  • Microservices Architectures

    • Its speed and interoperability make it a go-to for communication between microservices.
    • Streaming capabilities shine in apps needing live data, such as financial dashboards, sports updates, or collaborative tools.
    • For performance-critical internal APIs within an organization, gRPC’s efficiency and typing are hard to beat.
    • Teams using diverse languages benefit from gRPC’s ability to bridge tech stacks effortlessly.

gRPC is a formidable framework for crafting efficient, high-performance APIs, especially in distributed systems like microservices or real-time applications. Its use of Protocol Buffers, HTTP/2, and streaming sets it apart from REST, offering a modern approach to API design. However, its complexity and specific constraints—like browser compatibility—mean it’s not always the simplest choice.

By weighing its features, advantages, and challenges, you can decide if gRPC aligns with your project’s goals. For scenarios demanding speed, scalability, and real-time interaction, gRPC might just be the perfect tool in your API toolkit.

items.title: How CargoWise Neo Improves Freight Forwarding Efficiency
items.author: Muthu Kumar
items.content:
Efficiency is key to survival in the competitive world of freight forwarding and logistics. Businesses are required to handle intricate supply chain operations while facilitating smooth communication, real-time monitoring, and adhesion to international trade regulations. CargoWise Neo is a cloud-based logistics solution aimed at streamlining freight forwarding by offering end-to-end automation, visibility, and collaboration capabilities. The following article discusses how CargoWise Neo boosts efficiency in freight forwarding and enables logistics businesses to remain competitive.

1. Streamlining Booking and Order Management

One of the greatest challenges freight forwarders have is juggling multiple bookings and orders from customers, but CargoWise Neo streamlines this by providing one centralized platform for booking shipments, managing orders, and confirming schedules in real time. With automated workflows, companies can cut down manual data entry, error rates, and boost overall productivity.

2. Enhancing Real-Time Shipment Tracking

Visibility is key to freight forwarders since customers need to be able to follow their shipments at each level. Real-time tracking with updates on cargo movement, estimated time of arrival, and possible delays are made possible through . Through the ability to connect with several carriers and logistics providers, the system allows for smooth data synchronization, which enables customers to make accurate decisions and streamline their supply chain procedures.

3. Improving Communication and Collaboration

Good communication among customers, shippers, and freight forwarders is vital for streamlined operations. CargoWise Neo enables smooth cooperation through its messaging and document exchange capabilities. It obviates the necessity of lengthy email chains by offering a single platform where all stakeholders can share updates, exchange required documentation, and handle issues effectively.

4. Automating Documentation and Compliance

Freight forwarding is a highly paper-intensive process, with bills of lading, customs declarations, and regulatory compliance documents. CargoWise Neo streamlines document creation, storage, and retrieval, minimizing administrative tasks and compliance with international trade regulations. Automated compliance checks enable companies to steer clear of penalties and simplify customs clearance, thereby accelerating the delivery process.

5. Optimizing Warehouse and Inventory Management

Effective warehouse operations are essential for freight forwarders who handle large cargo volumes. CargoWise Neo combines warehouse management capabilities, and businesses can track inventory levels, control stock flow, and improve storage space management. With live inventory tracking, companies can improve on-time deliveries, lower storage expenses, and increase order delivery accuracy.

6. Simplifying Financial and Billing Processes

Correct invoicing and financial openness are key to sustaining profitability in freight forwarding. CargoWise Neo offers automated billing options that create invoices, monitor payments, and reconcile accounts with ease. Companies can view financial reports in real time, ensuring correct revenue tracking and reducing the possibility of discrepancies.

7. Enhancing Customer Experience and Self-Service Capabilities

CargoWise Neo empowers clients through self-service functionality. Clients can access shipment information, inquire about quotes, download invoices, and book in their own time without reaching out to customer care. Self-service helps improve client satisfaction while offloading the freight forwarding team's workload.

8. Ensuring Seamless Integration with Existing Systems

Several logistics firms already employ different software solutions to manage their operations. CargoWise Neo is built with the ability to integrate with current enterprise resource planning (ERP), transportation management systems (TMS), and third-party applications. Such interoperability allows businesses to ensure data consistency and automate end-to-end logistics processes.

9. Data-Driven Decision-Making and Analytics

CargoWise Neo offers sophisticated analytics and reporting capabilities that enable logistics businesses to make informed decisions. Through the analysis of key performance indicators, companies can spot inefficiencies, streamline routes, and improve operational strategies. The data-driven insights of the platform facilitate ongoing improvement, resulting in improved cost control and profitability.

10. Boosting Scalability and Global Expansion

As freight forwarding businesses expand, they need scalable solutions to manage growing volumes of shipments and growing international operations. CargoWise Neo accommodates multi-location management, multi-currency transactions, and adherence to varied international trade regulations. Its cloud-based infrastructure allows businesses to scale their operations effectively without the need to invest in expensive infrastructure.

CargoWise Neo is revolutionary for freight forwarders who want to optimize efficiency, lower operational expenses, and maximize customer satisfaction. Through its automation, real-time visibility, built-in communication capabilities, and data-driven analytics, the solution allows logistics businesses to remain at the forefront of a competitive business. With CargoWise Neo, freight forwarders can attain seamless business operations, streamline supply chains, and build long-term business prosperity.

items.title: Which Technology to Use to Develop an App Like Spotify?
items.author: Gaurav Kanabar
items.content:
Streaming apps like Spotify have revolutionized how we consume music, offering seamless access to millions of tracks on demand. The success of Spotify lies not only in its massive music library but also in the cutting-edge technology that powers its smooth user experience, fast content delivery, and intelligent music recommendations.

If you’re planning to develop a music streaming app like Spotify, understanding the underlying technologies and frameworks is essential. In this blog, we’ll explore the key technologies, tools, and best practices you’ll need to create a robust and scalable music streaming app.

Key Features of a Music Streaming App Like Spotify

Before diving into the technology stack, it’s crucial to define the core features that make an app like Spotify successful:

– Ability to store, organize, and categorize millions of tracks.User Profiles and Playlists – Allow users to create, modify, and share playlists. – Fast and buffer-free audio streaming. – An Option to download songs for offline playback.Intelligent Recommendations – Personalized playlists and suggestions based on listening behavior. – Allow users to follow friends and share music. – Seamless experience across mobile, desktop, and web.Data Security and Privacy – Secure user data and payment information.

Best Technologies to Develop an App Like Spotify

To build a scalable, high-performance music streaming app like Spotify, you need to choose the right mix of programming languages, frameworks, and cloud infrastructure. Here's a detailed breakdown of the essential technologies:

The front end is the user-facing side of the app where the design, navigation, and user interactions happen. It needs to be fast, responsive, and visually appealing.

– A popular cross-platform framework for developing mobile apps for both iOS and Android using JavaScript. It allows for fast development and reusable code across platforms. – Google's framework using Dart, known for its high performance and rich UI components.

– Ideal for building a responsive and dynamic web interface with reusable components. – Lightweight and flexible for building interactive user interfaces. – Suitable for complex web applications with two-way data binding and modular architecture.

– Used for creating cross-platform desktop applications using web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript).

The backend is responsible for handling music data, user accounts, payments, and streaming services. It processes user requests and ensures smooth communication between the client and the server.

– Used for data processing, machine learning, and managing complex backend logic. – Lightweight and efficient for handling asynchronous operations and real-time streaming. – Well-suited for scalable and secure applications with complex logic.

– A high-level framework that encourages rapid Spotify clone development and clean architecture. – Minimalist framework for handling RESTful APIs and web requests. – A powerful framework for building microservices and secure APIs.

A music streaming app requires a high-performance database to store user data, music metadata, and listening history.

Relational Databases (for structured data): – Open-source and powerful for handling complex queries and transactions. – Reliable for structured data storage with strong community support.

NoSQL Databases (for unstructured data): – Ideal for storing flexible data models like playlists and user preferences. – Known for handling large amounts of data with high availability.

Cloud Storage and Content Delivery A scalable cloud solution is essential for storing music files and delivering them to users quickly.

– Secure and cost-effective cloud storage for music files. – It offers global coverage and fast data delivery.Content Delivery Network (CDN) – Services like Cloudflare and Akamai for reducing latency and improving streaming speed.

Spotify relies on specialized streaming protocols to ensure smooth and uninterrupted audio playback.

HTTP Live Streaming (HLS) – Efficient for delivering audio and video streams over the internet.Real-Time Messaging Protocol (RTMP) – Used for low-latency streaming.Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP (DASH) – Adjusts streaming quality based on network conditions.

Machine Learning and Recommendation Algorithms Personalized recommendations and playlists are key differentiators for Spotify. Machine learning models analyze user behavior and preferences to suggest relevant content.

– Ideal for developing machine learning models. – Google’s open-source machine learning platform. – A Python library for data mining and analysis. – Recommends content based on user similarity.Natural Language Processing (NLP) – Analyzes song lyrics and user reviews to improve recommendations.

User Authentication and Security A secure login and payment process is critical to protect user data.

– For secure login using social media accounts. – For session-based authentication. – For encrypted data transmission. – For secure payment processing.

Push Notifications and Real-Time Updates Engage users and keep them updated with personalized notifications.

Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) – Google’s platform for push notifications. – A popular tool for handling mobile and web push notifications.

Best Practices for Developing a Music Streaming App 🔹 Focus on User Experience: Ensure a smooth, lag-free experience with intuitive navigation. 🔹 Optimize for Scalability: Use microservices architecture to handle increased traffic. 🔹 Encrypt user data and comply with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR). 🔹 Use global CDNs to reduce buffering times. 🔹 Use A/B testing to optimize features and user engagement.

Building an app like Spotify requires a robust technology stack and a strategic approach to handle complex data processing, real-time streaming, and personalized recommendations.

By choosing the right programming languages, frameworks, and cloud solutions, you can create a high-performance music streaming app that offers a seamless listening experience and engages users effectively.

Invest in the right technology, and you’ll be on your way to developing a music streaming app that rivals Spotify in both functionality and user satisfaction.

items.title: Why is Sovereign Cloud Important?
items.author: Shivam Chamoli
items.content:
Have you ever wondered how organizations manage their data, applications, and infrastructure in the cloud environment? Or how do they verify that data stays within a certain geographic boundary? Or how do they ensure they comply with local laws and regulations? The answer to all this is Sovereign Cloud. As data becomes a crucial asset driving decision-making, innovation, and growth, businesses face increasing concerns over security, compliance, and regulatory adherence. Sovereign Cloud helps keep data within national borders, ensuring it follows local laws and stays protected from unauthorized access. It helps businesses reduce risks, stay in control, comply with data protection laws, and earn customer trust. With evolving cyber threats and geopolitical uncertainties, adopting a Sovereign Cloud strategy is essential for safeguarding data and maintaining operational efficiency.

What is a Sovereign Cloud?

A Sovereign Cloud is a specialized cloud solution designed to ensure that data is stored, processed, and managed within the boundaries of a specific country. It follows local laws and regulations, giving organizations full control over sensitive information. This is particularly crucial for industries such as healthcare, finance, and government services, where safeguarding data and adhering to strict compliance standards are imperative.

Why Sovereign Cloud Matters

1. Regulatory Compliance:

Governments enforce strict data sovereignty and residency laws like GDPR in Europe or CCPA in California. Sovereign Cloud helps organizations meet these requirements by keeping sensitive data within local borders and following regional regulations. This ensures businesses stay compliant and avoid hefty penalties.

2. Enhanced Data Security:

Data stored in foreign countries can be exposed to unauthorized access or surveillance under those nations’ laws. Sovereign Cloud tackles this issue by keeping data within the country and limiting access to authorized individuals, offering stronger privacy and protection.

3. Mitigating Geopolitical Risks:

Global conflicts and sanctions can disrupt access to data stored abroad. Sovereign Cloud solves this by keeping essential data localized, reducing reliance on foreign systems, and ensuring business operations remain uninterrupted during geopolitical conflicts.

4. Boosting National Digital Infrastructure:

Sovereign Cloud drives the growth of local digital ecosystems by encouraging investment in domestic data centers, cloud services, and cybersecurity. This strengthens a nation’s infrastructure, fosters innovation in cloud technology, and creates opportunities for local businesses.

Key Benefits of Sovereign Cloud

Sovereign Cloud gives organizations complete control over their data, making it easier to comply with industry-specific regulations. This reduces the chances of unauthorized access, data breaches, or external interference, providing peace of mind and greater accountability.

2. Trust and Transparency:

Using a Sovereign Cloud helps businesses build trust with clients and partners by prioritizing data privacy and security. It reassures stakeholders that sensitive information is handled responsibly and complies with local laws, enhancing credibility.

Sovereign Cloud offers customized services that cater to a country’s unique legal, cultural, and economic needs. This localized approach ensures businesses and users experience smooth operations without compromising compliance or functionality.

By keeping data within national borders, Sovereign Cloud enhances operational resilience. Organizations can avoid disruptions caused by foreign data dependencies, ensuring uninterrupted access and recovery during crises or external challenges.

CCAK Training with InfosecTrain

The Sovereign Cloud is a strategic necessity, enabling organizations to comply with regulations, enhance security, and mitigate risks while building trust. Certifications like CCAK certification training from CSA and ISACA equip professionals to audit and secure cloud environments effectively. Paired with CISA certification training, CISM, or ISO 27001, CCAK helps implement robust cloud strategies. InfosecTrain provides expert-led training on CCAK and related certifications, empowering professionals to embrace secure digital transformation. Sovereign Cloud solutions safeguard data and ensure resilience in a fragmented digital landscape.

items.title: Simple way to deal with race conditions in Rails
items.author: Augusts Bautra
items.content:
Wrap all .first_or_create!/find_or_create_by! calls in retryable block.

Oftentimes in Rails apps we have a situation where only one of something is allowed. A user may be allowed only one profile picture, a question only one answer, only one pinned post etc.

In these situations I prefer using the "ensure" pattern with .first_or_create!/find_or_create_by!. There's a gotcha, however. Both of these methods are vulnerable to race conditions, especially if the ensuring logic is called in GET action context (though POSTs that do not disable to submit button may also suffer from this).

What happens is several near-parallel requests reach the , and the check for any existing records returns false for both, so both proceed to creation, but only one will succeed in this, and the other will fail.

While we should strive to reduce unnecessary requests by disabling submit buttons after click etc., oftentimes the source of requests is not under our control - modern browsers tend to make pre-fetch requests etc., so handling the race condition is a must. Luckily this is easy.

I like retryable gem a lot for this, it has a clean, powerful DSL.

Then wrap any .first_or_create!/find_or_create_by! call in a retrying block. By default it will retry just once and wait one second after a failure if there is one, you can tweak all that.

items.title: 10 Essential Tools for Modern Developers 🚀
items.author: Daniel Markson
items.content:
With new technologies evolving rapidly, developers are adopting advanced tools to streamline their workflow, enhance productivity, and deliver high-quality code faster. Here are 10 essential tools that can elevate your coding experience:

  1. Notion is an all-in-one workspace that combines note-taking, project management, and collaboration. It’s a great tool for organizing tasks, managing documentation, and storing important resources for your development projects.
  1. SourceTree is a free Git GUI that simplifies version control. It provides a clean interface to manage repositories, making Git easier to navigate and helping you visualize your changes.
  1. Trello is a visual collaboration tool that organizes your tasks using boards, lists, and cards. It’s perfect for keeping track of deadlines, sprint planning, and team workflows in an easy-to-use interface.

  1. Slack is an essential tool for team communication and collaboration. With channels, direct messaging, and integration with tools like GitHub and Jira, it keeps your team connected and aligned in real-time.

  2. NVM allows you to easily switch between different versions of Node.js. It simplifies managing and testing code with multiple Node.js versions, ensuring compatibility and smooth development.

  1. Webpack is a powerful module bundler for JavaScript applications. It optimizes your code for production, minifying files and bundling assets, ensuring faster load times and better performance.

  1. Swagger is a suite of tools for designing, building, and documenting APIs. It simplifies API development with its user-friendly interface and auto-generated documentation, making it easier for developers to create robust and scalable APIs.

  2. CircleCI automates the process of continuous integration and deployment. It streamlines testing and deployment workflows, reducing manual effort and accelerating the delivery of code changes.

  1. GitKraken is a Git GUI that offers an intuitive interface for managing repositories. With features like visual commit history and drag-and-drop merge conflict resolution, GitKraken makes version control more efficient and less error-prone.
  1. Linode is a cloud hosting provider that’s perfect for developers who need fast, scalable cloud infrastructure. Whether you’re deploying a web app or setting up a development environment, Linode offers flexible solutions at competitive prices.

Integrating these tools into your development workflow will not only help you streamline processes but also boost your productivity. Whether it’s managing projects, automating tasks, or optimizing code, these tools are built to help you work smarter and faster.

items.title: Key Features of Amazon GuardDuty
items.author: Shivam Chamoli
items.content:
Amazon GuardDuty is a fully managed threat detection service designed to monitor, analyze, and detect potential security threats across AWS environments. It continuously ingests and analyzes data from key AWS sources like CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs, leveraging advanced machine learning, anomaly detection, and third-party threat intelligence feeds to identify unauthorized activities, malicious behavior, and other security risks.

Whether identifying insider threats, malware, or external attacks, GuardDuty serves as a critical component in maintaining a secure and compliant cloud environment.

Key Features of Amazon GuardDuty

Amazon GuardDuty key features include:

1. Continuous Monitoring and Analysis

• GuardDuty ingests data from AWS CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS query logs to provide deep visibility into account activity and network traffic. • Uses machine learning, statistical analysis, and pattern matching to detect suspicious behavior or anomalies, such as unusual login attempts, port scanning, or access to unusual resources. • Threat Detection Capabilities: This capability identifies multi-stage attacks that span multiple AWS resources and time frames, detecting complex threat sequences that may not be apparent when events are viewed in isolation.

2. GuardDuty Protection Plans

GuardDuty offers specialized protection plans that can be enabled to monitor additional AWS services: • Detects data exfiltration and unauthorized modifications. • Monitors Kubernetes audit logs for malicious activities. • Detects OS-level threats on EKS, EC2, and ECS (including Fargate). • Scans EC2 (EBS volumes) and S3 for malware. • Identifies suspicious login activity on Aurora and RDS. • Detects unauthorized network activity and crypto mining attempts.

3. Intelligent Threat Identification

• Leverages ML algorithms to adapt and refine its detection models based on emerging threats and customer-specific patterns. • Threat Intelligence Feeds: Integrates with AWS Threat Intelligence and third-party feeds to detect known malicious IPs and domains.

• Generates detailed findings that specify the nature of the threat, affected resources, and remediation steps. • Assigns severity levels (low, medium, high) to findings for prioritization.

5. Integration with AWS Security Services:

Seamlessly connects with other AWS services to enhance incident response and remediation like: • Assists in analyzing and investigating potential security issues. • Provides a comprehensive view of your security state across AWS accounts. • Enables real-time monitoring and automated responses to security findings.

Explore more articles on AWS.

AWS Combo Training with InfosecTrain

To deepen your understanding of Amazon GuardDuty and AWS security, enroll in InfosecTrain's AWS Combo Training course. Gain hands-on experience, explore practical use cases, and master advanced concepts to enhance your cloud security expertise and career opportunities.

items.title: Improving Maven's dependency:analyze... or not
items.author: Nicolas Fränkel
items.content:
Recently, my good friend Richard Fichtner advised using the command to get rid of declared but unused dependencies:

While it was a great idea years ago, it's dangerous advice today. In this post, I'd like to explain what the plugin does and why you shouldn't use it but in the most straightforward projects.

The command

Maven uses a plugin architecture; in the above command, the plugin is maven-dependency-plugin. A plugin hosts several related . Here, it's .

Analyzes the dependencies of this project and determines which are: used and declared; used and undeclared; unused and declared. This goal is intended to be used standalone, thus it always executes the phase - use the goal instead when participating in the build lifecycle.

By default, maven-dependency-analyzer is used to perform the analysis, with limitations due to the fact that it works at bytecode level, but any analyzer can be plugged in through parameter.

maven-dependency-analyzer is a shared Maven component. Its description is quite descriptive:

Analyzes the dependencies of a project for undeclared or unused artifacts.

: Because analysis is done on the bytecode rather than the source, some cases are not detected including constants, annotations with source-only retention, and links in Javadoc. This can lead to incorrect results when these are the only uses of a dependency.

The main component is ProjectDependencyAnalyzer, which uses and .

The warning clearly shows that it works at the level. In particular, it explicitly mentions that it doesn't consider source-level annotations.

SpringBoot relies on AutoConfiguration classes. AutoConfiguration classes are regular configuration classes, , they contribute to the application classes. You can set specific activation criteria, such as the presence of a Spring property, but these are not specific to auto-configuration.

Here's a very simplified flow:

The JAR that automatically comes with Spring Boot is org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-autoconfigure. You can check the content of its META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports:

...
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.client.RestClientAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.client.RestTemplateAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.embedded.EmbeddedWebServerFactoryCustomizerAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.HttpHandlerAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.ReactiveMultipartAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.ReactiveWebServerFactoryAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.WebFluxAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.WebSessionIdResolverAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.error.ErrorWebFluxAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.function.client.ClientHttpConnectorAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.reactive.function.client.WebClientAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.DispatcherServletAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.ServletWebServerFactoryAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.error.ErrorMvcAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.HttpEncodingAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.MultipartAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.web.servlet.WebMvcAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.websocket.reactive.WebSocketReactiveAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.websocket.servlet.WebSocketServletAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.websocket.servlet.WebSocketMessagingAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.webservices.WebServicesAutoConfiguration
org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.webservices.client.WebServiceTemplateAutoConfiguration

As an example, here's the RestClientAutoConfiguration:

  1. Set the order of auto-configuration classes
  2. Activate if the class is on the classpath
  3. Activate if we aren't in a reactive web app context

Note that the class loader loads the RestTemplateAutoConfiguration class just fine, regardless of whether the class is on the classpath or not! Spring leverages this mechanism to its fullest, as seen above. In effect, the resolution of classes configured in annotations is deferred until they are accessed.

Bringing the maven-dependency-analyzer into the modern age

Committers designed the analyzer in 2007: here's how it looked like then. Spring Boot started later, in 2010. For this reason, the analyzer didn't take deferred class loading in annotations. Note that this is still not the case; the project doesn't get a lot of love.

When using the plugin on a Spring Boot project, you'll get a lot of false positives. I tried it with a simple Spring Boot project, using WebFlux and R2DBC on PostgreSQL.

Here's a slight excerpt of the output when I run :

[WARNING] Unused declared dependencies found:
[WARNING]   org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-data-r2dbc:jar:3.4.0:compile
[WARNING]   org.testcontainers:postgresql:jar:1.20.4:test
[WARNING]   org.testcontainers:r2dbc:jar:1.20.4:test

If I remove any of these dependencies, tests don't run.

What would be necessary to make the analyzer work with Spring Boot projects? Let's analyze the analyzer.

The plugin allows configuring another analyzer:

Specify the project dependency analyzer to use (plexus component role-hint). By default, maven-dependency-analyzer is used. To use this, you must declare a dependency for this plugin that contains the code for the analyzer. The analyzer must have a declared Plexus role name, and you specify the role name here.

    We can create an overall analyzer that reuses the above but adds one specific to Spring Boot.

    The current state of the Maven analyzer doesn't offer any benefit to modern Spring Boot projects. The existing code is open to configuration and even extension. However, we would need to embed a lot of Spring Boot logic. For Quarkus and Micronaut projects, we would require dedicated code as well.

    I don't know if it's worth the time and effort. If you think it is, I hope this blog post can serve as an early-stage analysis.

items.title: Unveiling the Libpng License: A Deep Dive into Open Source Fairness
items.author: Rachel Duncan
items.content:
The world of open source licensing is evolving, and the Libpng License summary offers an insightful look into a unique legal framework designed for the libpng library. In this blog post, we explore the origins, evolution, and practical implications of this license, discussing how its balanced approach has played a critical role in fostering innovation while also raising important questions about fairness and developer compensation.

The Libpng License was developed to support the libpng library—an essential component for handling PNG images in a wide range of applications. With roots in the early days of digital image processing, the license was carefully crafted to provide broad usability with minimal legal complexities. Over time, this permissive license has not only allowed seamless integration into both open source and proprietary projects but has also served as a benchmark for fairness in the open source community. At its core, the Libpng License aims to blend simplicity with effective protection, ensuring that developers can integrate the library into their projects without facing heavy legal restrictions. This philosophy is echoed by many in the community who advocate for open source and fair code licenses. For additional perspectives on open source licensing, be sure to check out OSI Licenses and the detailed MIT License FAQ.

The comprehensive review of the Libpng License breaks down its various dimensions from historical origins to modern-day challenges. Initially devised to protect and promote the development of libpng, the license focuses on minimizing legal overhead while still providing fundamental safeguards for developers. Its simplicity has helped drive the adoption in critical projects such as the Apache HTTP Server and components of the Linux Kernel.

  1. Balanced Legal Framework: The Libpng License adopts a middle path between permissiveness and protection. It emphasizes ease of integration, presenting a less restrictive alternative compared to rigid copyleft licenses. However, critics argue that its model, which leans heavily on community goodwill and donations, might leave room for exploitation by commercial entities.
  2. Tracing back to the early PNG format development, the license emerged as the developers needed a tool that would empower collaboration without demanding extensive legal commitments. Over time, its clear documentation and stability became a cornerstone in open source licensing debates, influencing discussions on platforms like Stack Overflow and Hacker News.
  3. Challenges and Future Directions: While the Libpng License offers notable benefits, its limitations regarding explicit compensation mechanisms have led to ongoing debates. With rising interest in blockchain-based solutions, such as those highlighted in the OCTL Whitepaper, there is growing pressure to adapt license frameworks to better protect developers’ contributions and ensure fair remuneration.
  4. Community and Developer Impact: The open source community highly values the transparency and legal simplicity of the Libpng License. Yet, instances of unattributed contributions and the absence of comprehensive Contributor License Agreements (CLAs) have highlighted vulnerabilities that could lead to disputes and legal ambiguity. Community feedback from Reddit and expert discussions on GitHub emphasize the need for continual improvement. By benchmarking itself against licenses like the MIT License and the Apache 2.0 License, the Libpng License summary not only stands out for its historical significance but also invites open source developers to reconsider how new legal tools can further balance innovation with developer rights.

The story of the Libpng License is one of innovation, balance, and continuous evolution. Its ability to integrate seamlessly into a range of projects, from multimedia applications to web servers, underscores its importance in the tech ecosystem. However, the challenges it faces—especially regarding commercial exploitation and insufficient compensation mechanisms—serve as a reminder that even well-regarded legal frameworks must evolve with changing technological landscapes. In reflecting on the Libpng License and its broader implications, open source advocates and developers are encouraged to engage in ongoing dialogue. As we look to the future, exploring alternative models like blockchain-based licensing systems may offer promising routes to safeguard the contributions of creators. For those interested in learning more about fair code practices and further details on licensing debates, visiting license-token.com is a great next step. Embracing transparent discussions around licenses like these not only fuels innovation but also ensures that the developers who power our digital world receive the fairness they deserve. Happy coding, and may fairness and clarity continue to guide the evolution of open source licensing!

items.title: Minimal APIs and Middleware in ASP.NET Core
items.author: Spyros Ponaris
items.content:
Minimal APIs and Middleware in ASP.NET Core

ASP.NET Core Minimal APIs let you build simple and fast web APIs with less code. Middleware helps process requests and responses, adding features like logging, authentication, and error handling.

In this guide, you'll learn how to set up a Minimal API and use middleware effectively.

A Short History of Middleware

Middleware has been used in web applications for a long time. In the past, web apps handled requests in one big block of code. Over time, middleware was introduced to make request handling more modular and flexible.

  • Classic ASP & Early .NET: Used HTTP modules and handlers.
  • ASP.NET MVC & Web API (2010s): Introduced delegating handlers for request processing.
  • ASP.NET Core (2016): Introduced a fully modular middleware pipeline.
  • ASP.NET Core Minimal APIs (2021+): Made middleware even simpler and lighter.

Prerequisites

Make sure you have .NET 8 SDK installed. Check by running:

Creating a Minimal API Project

dotnet new web -o MinimalApiDemo cd MinimalApiDemo

Open Program.cs and you'll see this simple API:

var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);
var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/", () => "Hello, World!");

app.Run();

Middleware is a set of steps that process each request and response. It can:

  • Handle authentication and security

Example of simple logging middleware:

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine($"Request: {context.Request.Method} {context.Request.Path}");
    await next();
    Console.WriteLine($"Response: {context.Response.StatusCode}");
});

Creating Custom Middleware

Instead of writing middleware inside Program.cs, we can create a separate class.

Example: Logging Middleware

Create LoggingMiddleware.cs:

public class LoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;

    public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
    {
        _next = next;
    }

    public async Task Invoke(HttpContext context)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"Incoming: {context.Request.Method} {context.Request.Path}");
        await _next(context);
        Console.WriteLine($"Outgoing: {context.Response.StatusCode}");
    }
}

Register it in Program.cs:

app.UseMiddleware<LoggingMiddleware>();

Handling Errors with Middleware

To catch errors globally, add this middleware:

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    try
    {
        await next();
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        context.Response.StatusCode = 500;
        await context.Response.WriteAsync($"Error: {ex.Message}");
    }
});

Adding Authentication Middleware

To use JWT authentication, install the package:

dotnet add package Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer

builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        options.Authority = "https://your-auth-server.com";
        options.Audience = "your-api-audience";
    });

app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapGet("/secure", () => "Secure data").RequireAuthorization();

Advanced Example: Rate Limiting Middleware

using Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting;
using System.Threading.RateLimiting;

var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(_ => _
    .AddFixedWindowLimiter(policyName: "fixed", options =>
    {
        options.PermitLimit = 4;
        options.Window = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(12);
        options.QueueProcessingOrder = QueueProcessingOrder.OldestFirst;
        options.QueueLimit = 2;
    }));

var app = builder.Build();

app.UseRateLimiter();

static string GetTicks() => (DateTime.Now.Ticks & 0x11111).ToString("00000");

app.MapGet("/", () => Results.Ok($"Hello {GetTicks()}"))
                           .RequireRateLimiting("fixed");

app.Run();

Best Practices for Middleware

  • Order matters: Middleware runs in the order you add it.
  • Handle errors properly: Always catch unexpected errors.
  • Keep it simple: Middleware should be lightweight.
  • Use dependency injection: Avoid static classes for middleware.

Minimal APIs in ASP.NET Core make it easy to build lightweight web applications. Middleware helps you add features like logging, security, and error handling in a clean and reusable way.

Try creating your own middleware for caching, validation, or performance monitoring!

items.title: Unit Testing with Coroutines in Kotlin: Tools and Best Practices
items.author: Alan Gomes
items.content:
Testing asynchronous code can be challenging. Functions that use coroutines can have unpredictable behavior due to delays, concurrent execution, and context switches. Fortunately, Kotlin provides built-in tools and helper libraries to simplify unit testing with coroutines .

In this article, we will explore how to use tools like , , and the library to efficiently test coroutines and flows.

2 – The Problem of Testing Asynchronous Code

Testing asynchronous code is tricky because:

  1. Coroutines can run in different threads or contexts.
  2. Methods like or introduce real waits that can slow down tests.
  3. Flows depend on asynchronous events that need to be controlled.

Without the right tools, tests can become flaky or take longer than necessary.

3.1 – UnconfinedTestDispatcher

  • A dispatcher designed for testing that is not tied to specific threads.
  • Allows you to run coroutines without context restrictions, making tests predictable.

3.2 – TestCoroutineScheduler

  • A scheduler that allows you to manually advance time in tests.
  • To control time-based tasks (, ) without waiting for real time to pass.
  • The annotation is used to mark a method as a , allowing it to be executed by JUnit.
  • Without , the testCoroutines method would be treated as a normal method and would not appear as executable in the IDE.
  • When JUnit detects , it:
    1. Automatically instantiate the test class.
    2. Execute the marked method, with all dependencies configured (such as and ).
  • A function that provides a controlled environment for running coroutines in tests.
  • Simplifies the setup of asynchronous tests by replacing in tests.

4 - Testing flows with the turbine library

For Flows, the Turbine library simplifies the collection and validation of emitted values.

Turbine is a library designed specifically for testing flows in Kotlin , allowing you to validate emitted items and events like cancellation or completion.

Runs coroutines without context restrictions.Simple tests that do not rely on real-time.
Manually controls timing in tests.Tests with , , or time events.
Tests values emitted by .

Testing coroutines and flows in Kotlin can be challenging, but with the right tools, you can create fast, efficient, and reliable tests. Using , , and libraries like Turbine makes the process much simpler.

  1. : Use TestCoroutineScheduler to manage delays and scheduling.
  2. : runTest is the basis for asynchronous testing.
  3. : Turbine is the ideal solution for validating flows.

Now that you know the best practices for testing coroutines in Kotlin , try applying them to your projects!

In the next article, we will discuss a little more about examples of asynchrony, just to explore the subject a little more and fix the content better.
